goalDetails.detail: Enhance, protect, and preserve distinct communities.

policies.policyDescription: Distinct Communities

subPolicies.description: Encourage quality development that is consistent with the distinct character of communities.

subPolicies.text: See Goal 4.3 below for descriptions of character-defining elements for each City CPA.
See Land Use Policy 5.2.1 for desired land uses.
See Urban Design Policies 7.3.2 and 7.3.3 for policies on community character and placemaking.
See Housing Goal 9.1 for policies related to housing options and affordability.

policies.policyDescription: Identity and Design

subPolicies.description: Protect the identity and cohesiveness of neighborhoods by ensuring the appropriate scale and location of development, mix of uses, and character of building design.

subPolicies.text: Maintain and preserve the unique qualities of historic areas. [ABC]
See County Area and Sector Development Plans for guidance in the County. [BC]
See Goal 4.2 below for the process to identify community character.
See Goal 4.3 below for City CPA character considerations for development.
See Land Use Goal 5.2 for policies about Complete Communities.
See Land Use Goals 5.5 and 5.6 for Development Area policies.

policies.policyDescription: Placemaking: Protect and enhance special places in the built environment that contribute to distinct identity and sense of place. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Urban Design Policies 7.3.2 and 7.3.3 for policies on community character and placemaking.
See Heritage Conservation chapter for historic and cultural considerations.

policies.policyDescription: Neighborhoods: Enhance, protect, and preserve neighborhoods and traditional communities as key to our long-term health and vitality. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Respect existing neighborhood values and social, cultural, recreational resources.
Leverage community resources to identify issues, opportunities, and special places and promote strong community identity.
Support improvements that protect stable, thriving residential neighborhoods and enhance their attractiveness.
Encourage transformative change in neighborhoods expressing the desire for revitalization.
See Land Use Policy 5.2.1 for guidance about where certain land uses are appropriate.
See Land Use Policies 5.6.3 and 5.6.4 for policies about Areas of Consistency and how to transition between Areas of Change and Consistency.
See Land Use Goal 5.7 for policies to promote public-private partnerships for catalytic development in Centers and Corridors.
See Housing Policy 9.7.2 for Metropolitan Redevelopment.
See Heritage Conservation Policy 11.2.1 for minimizing the negative impacts of gentrification on communities.
See Implementation Strategic Action 3.3 for catalytic projects.

policies.policyDescription: Natural Resources: Encourage high-quality development and redevelopment that responds appropriately to the natural setting and ecosystem functions. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Respect natural environmental conditions and carrying capacities and cultural landscapes when locating new development and regulating its intensity and design.
See Urban Design Goal 7.5 for context-sensitive site design.
See Heritage Conservation Goal 11.1 for policies to protect rural and agricultural heritage.
See Heritage Conservation Goal 11.3 for policies to protect cultural landscapes.
See Resilience & Sustainability Goal 13.4 for policies to protect natural resources.

goalDetails.detail: Engage communities to identify and plan for their distinct character and needs.

policies.policyDescription: Community Planning Areas: Use Community Planning Areas to track conditions and progress toward implementation of the community vision over time and organize planning efforts to identify distinct community character. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Guide development through Comp Plan Development Area policies in Land Use Goals 5.5 and 5.6. [ABC]
Use County Area or Sector Development Plans to further guide development. [BC]
Use policies in Community Identity Goal 4.3 to further guide development in City CPAs. [A]
See Land Use Policy 5.3.7 to address objectionable land uses.
See Resilience & Sustainability Policy 13.5.4 to address potential adverse impacts of development.

policies.policyDescription: Community Engagement: Facilitate meaningful engagement opportunities and respectful interactions in order to identify and address the needs of all residents. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Engage communities to help identify, build, and strengthen distinct identity and sense of community. [A]
Increase awareness about and understanding of cultural differences, shared identity, and differing needs across communities. [A]
Build capacity for more culturally significant interactions between City and County staff and the public. [A]
Work with community leaders to identify and remove barriers to meaningful community engagement. [A]
Create robust and meaningful public involvement processes to help build long-term consensus about growth and development in the Albuquerque area. [A]
See Land Use Policy 5.7.5 for additional policies on public engagement.
See Transportation Goal 6.5 for policies about equity in transportation systems.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.4.1 for collaborative strategies to prioritize public investment.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.4.2 for policies about ADA accessibility in public facilities.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policies 12.5.5 and 12.5.6 for policies about staff capacity and public input in public resource allocation.

goalDetails.detail: Protect and enhance the natural and cultural characteristics and features that contribute to distinct identity and prioritize projects and programs to meet the needs of communities, neighborhoods, and sub-areas. [A]

policies.policyDescription: CPA Assessments: Identify the character-defining elements, priorities for capital investment, and potential programs and partnerships for each CPA through the ongoing, long-range planning assessment process. [A]

subPolicies.text: See Policy 4.2.1 above for policies and actions related to the CPA assessment process.
See Policies 4.3.2 through 4.3.13 below for policies specific to each CPA.
See Heritage Conservation chapter for historic and cultural considerations.

policies.policyDescription: Central Albuquerque CPA [TO BE COMPLETED THROUGH THE CPA ASSESSMENT PROCESS.]
East Gateway CPA [TO BE COMPLETED THROUGH THE CPA ASSESSMENT PROCESS.]
Foothills CPA [TO BE COMPLETED THROUGH THE CPA ASSESSMENT PROCESS.]
Mesa del Sol CPA [TO BE COMPLETED THROUGH THE CPA ASSESSMENT PROCESS.]
Mid Heights CPA [TO BE COMPLETED THROUGH THE CPA ASSESSMENT PROCESS.]
Near Heights CPA
Prioritize projects that increase the urban tree canopy, street trees, and green space, particularly in areas more than a 10-minute walk to a park. [A]

subPolicies.text: Create new parks and open space in areas more than a 10-minute walk to a park.
Encourage investment in street trees, landscaped medians, and landscape strips along streets, especially in areas more than a 10-minute walk to a park where new parks are not feasible.

Encourage edible landscaping, pesticide-free practices, and pollinator and migratory bird habitat in parks, public gathering places, and community facilities to promote access to free food for people and wildlife
Support community gardens.

policies.policyDescription: Support projects that increase housing supply, broaden housing options, and add affordable housing. [A]

subPolicies.text: Encourage housing options that support all family types, lifestyles, household sizes, and household incomes.
Encourage development projects and housing programs for people throughout all life stages.

subLevels.description: Support housing projects that add rental opportunities.
Support housing projects that add homeownership opportunities.
Support higher-density housing projects near parks, schools, and community facilities.
Encourage investment in housing assistance for major repairs for seniors and low-income households.
Prioritize anti-displacement strategies, particularly in the International District.

Encourage accessory dwelling units (ADUs) as an appropriate strategy to add housing options, maintain naturally affordable housing, support multi-generational lifestyles, and balance opportunities for ownership and renting in Near Heights.

policies.policyDescription: Prioritize projects that reinforce a sense of place tied to history and culture. [A]

subPolicies.text: Prioritize transportation projects that increase safety and the comfort of pedestrians, cyclists, and transit users in neighborhoods and along transportation routes.
Invest in additional lighting along streets, alleys, and underpasses.
Encourage projects that improve the usability and safety of alleyways.
Encourage projects that impove sidewalks and street crossings to meet ADA standards.
Encourage projects that improve walkability and provide better opportunities for walking as a recreational activity throughout the Near Heights.
Support projects to improve pedestrian and bicylce safety, especially at intersections with Commuter Corridors.

Support projects that improve the design and operation of streets to slow traffic and increase safety, particularly on corridors with high crash rates or high numbers of conflicts with pedestrians and cyclists.

policies.policyDescription: Support projects that reinforce a sense of place tied to history and culture. [A]

subPolicies.text: Encourage public art created by local artists and celebrating local people and cultures.
Support development that embraces the multiculturalism of the International District.
Encourage signage in multiple languages that reflect the culture of local residents.
Balance the needs of UNM heritage and stability of established neighborhoods near UNM.
Support development projects and local businesses that create, promote, and maintain local identity and sense of place.
Encourage culturally relevant plant pallets with informational signage to reinforce a sense of place, storytelling, and heritage preservation.

Support development along the corridor that honors the Mid-Century aesthetic, supports small businesses that serve families in the surrounding area, and attract tourists and others from the broader community.
Encourage recognition of historic assets though educational materials and supporting efforts such as historic plaques and signage in historic districts.
Promote active streetscapes and Crime Prevention Through Environmental (CPTED) design standards that encourage �eyes on the street.�

policies.policyDescription: Prioritize support for local small businesses. [A]

subPolicies.text: Preserve the historic character of the Nob Hill commercial district, support the design of commercial/residential transition areas to provide easy access from neighborhoods, and protect the integrity of historic districts.
Focus resources on projects and programs that support entrepreneurship in diverse communities.

policies.policyDescription: Support projects and investment that bolster transit as a viable and efficient transportation option. [A]

subPolicies.text: Increase investment in connections between routes, increased bus frequency, and additional north/south routes.
Support free transit fares on transit to increase ridership, support underserved communities, and promote sustainability.
Support higher-density housing development within Premium Transit corridors and near transit stops and stations.
Encourage transit-oriented development.
Improve signage and other sources of information about transit, including translation into multiple languages and updates as routes and schedules change.

policies.policyDescription: Support projects that increase access to bathrooms and sanitation throughout the area. [A]

subPolicies.text: Invest in publicly-available bathrooms, showers, and handwashing stations.
Encourage safety and cleanliness in parks by expanding needle pick up and adding bathrooms and/or sanitation stations.

policies.policyDescription: Near North Valley CPA [TO BE COMPLETED THROUGH THE CPA ASSESSMENT PROCESS.]
North Albuquerque CPA [TO BE COMPLETED THROUGH THE CPA ASSESSMENT PROCESS.]
North I-25 CPA [TO BE COMPLETED THROUGH THE CPA ASSESSMENT PROCESS.]
Northwest Mesa CPA [TO BE COMPLETED THROUGH THE CPA ASSESSMENT PROCESS.]
Southwest Mesa CPA
Encourage safety in parks through park activation, events, and activities. [A]

subPolicies.text: Encourage safety and cleanliness in parks by expanding needle pick-up, adding bathrooms, and/or sanitation stations.
Support programming, community events, and other desired activities.

policies.policyDescription: Improve multi-modal transportation and infrastructure in the Southwest Mesa. [A]

subPolicies.text: Support projects that improve the design and operation of streets to slow traffic and increase safety, particularly on corridors with high crash rates or high numbers of conflicts with pedestrians and cyclists.
Prioritize transportation projects according to the HFIN, emphasizing investment first in high-fatality areas and underserved communities.
Encourage projects that improve sidewalks and street crossings to meet ADA standards.
Prioritize road maintenance, repair, and reconstruction according to the Pavement Condition Index.
The City, County, and State should continue to meet regularly and coordinate on projects that touch multiple jurisdictions.
Support transit service throughout the Southwest Mesa through budget allocation, development, and design.

policies.policyDescription: Provide services for people experiencing homelessness and avenues to permanent housing solutions. [A]

subPolicies.text: Invest in publicly-available bathrooms, showers, and sanitation stations.
Support projects and programs that create rental or homeownership opportunities for low-income individuals or families.
Invest in facilities and programs that provide shelter and/or resources for people experiencing homelessness.

policies.policyDescription: Support and increase dense and mixed-use housing options in the Southwest Mesa. [A]

subPolicies.text: Support housing projects and programs that add affordable homeownership opportunities.
Support housing projects and programs that add affordable rental opportunities.
Encourage housing options, programs, and development projects that support multigenerational living arrangements
Support higher-density housing projects along major thoroughfares.
Invest in housing assistance for major repairs for seniors and low-income households.
Encourage housing options and development projects that prioritize accessible and age-friendly design.
Retain the historic character and design features of motels along Central that convert to housing.

policies.policyDescription: Support development that promotes the unique identity of the Southwest Mesa. [A]

subPolicies.text: Support creative and cultural development and public art that reflects cultural and community priorities in Southwest Mesa.
Encourage signage, outreach, and education related to the cultures and languages of the people and history of the Southwest Mesa.
Support projects that create or expand space and opportunities for youth and young adults to learn, connect, and grow.

policies.policyDescription: Maintain the historic character of motor tourism along Route 66, especially neon signs. [A]
Encourage development that incorporates neighborhood-scale services. [A]

subPolicies.text: Oppose projects that are exclusively single-family residential in any zone district other than R-1.
Encourage zone changes to MX-L on corner lots.
Support development that brings commercial amenities closer to Residential zone districts.

policies.policyDescription: Encourage zone changes that allow intense mixed-use, commercial, or light industrial land uses on vacant land. [A]
Support business development, retail, and services, particularly childcare and family-friendly activities. [A]

goalDetails.detail: Grow as a community of strong Centers connected by a multi-modal network of Corridors.

policies.policyDescription: West Mesa CPA [TO BE COMPLETED THROUGH THE CPA ASSESSMENT PROCESS.]
Desired Growth: Capture regional growth in Centers and Corridors to help shape the built environment into a sustainable development pattern. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Create walkable places that provide opportunities to live, work, learn, shop, and play.
Structure capital investment and land use regulations to direct growth to Centers, in particular those in need of public and private reinvestment.

Encourage employment density, compact development, redevelopment, and infill in Centers and Corridors as the most appropriate areas to accommodate growth over time and discourage the need for development at the urban edge.

Encourage the development of multi-unit, multi-story apartments and mixed-use residential buildings in Downtown, Urban Centers, and Activity Centers to increase housing density and expand housing options and affordability.
Encourage platting with a range of residential lot sizes to support choice in housing and to meet the needs of all income levels.
Discourage the development of detached single-family housing as an inappropriate use in Centers and along Corridors.
Encourage residential infill in neighborhoods adjacent to Centers and Corridors to support transit ridership.
Encourage all new development, especially in designated Centers and Corridors, to address transit connections, linkages, and opportunities within the proposed development.
Locate industrial development in Employment Centers or in existing industrial zones within the I-25 and I-40 corridors.
Follow development form policies in Urban Design Policy 7.1.3, Table 7-3 for Centers, and Table 7-4 for Corridors.
See Policies 5.1.3-5.1.12 below for uses appropriate in each Center and Corridor.
See Policy 5.2.2 below for appropriate uses outside of Centers and Corridors.
See Goal 5.3 below for policies that promote efficient development patterns.
See Policies 5.5.1 and 5.6.1 below for community green space protection.
See Community Identity Goal 4.1 for policies to protect and enhance existing neighborhoods and Goal 4.3 for area-specific policies.
See Transportation Goals 6.1 and 6.2 for policies that guide development in the public right-of way.
See Urban Design Policy 7.1.1 for the hierarchy of Centers and Corridors policies.
See Housing Goal 9.3 for policies related to housing development.
See Parks & Open Space Goal 10.1 for parks and Open Space facilities.
See Heritage Conservation Policy 10.1.1 for preservation of agricultural heritage.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Goal 12.2 for policies about locating community facilities in Centers.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.5.3 for public investment in infrastructure to direct growth.
See Resilience & Sustainability Goal 13.5 for community health considerations.

policies.policyDescription: Development Areas: Direct more intense growth to Centers and Corridors and use Development Areas to establish and maintain appropriate density and scale of development within areas that should be more stable. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Goal 5.5 for County Development Areas.
See Goal 5.6 for City Development Areas.

policies.policyDescription: Downtown: Strengthen Downtown�s identity as a regional hub for the highest-intensity development, with concentrated job and commercial activity supported by the highest-density housing. [A]

subPolicies.text: Support pedestrian-oriented development.
Support mixed-use development.
Ensure opportunities for public and private employment, civic uses, and cultural and entertainment activities.
Ensure that there are multiple transportation options to access and circulate within Downtown.
Encourage plazas and other open spaces to provide an inviting atmosphere for pedestrians and support a diversity of uses.
Create an 18-hour Downtown that generates activity throughout the day and evening.
Minimize the potential negative impacts of Downtown development on abutting neighborhoods.
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for development form and priority street elements in Downtown.
See Economic Development Policy 8.1.1 for creating places that support economic development.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.3.4 for public safety service provision.

policies.policyDescription: Urban Centers: Create highly accessible and walkable Urban Centers that provide a range of employment opportunities and higher-density housing options. [A]

subPolicies.text: Encourage mixed-use development.
Encourage pedestrian-oriented design, transit-oriented development, and infrastructure improvements that make Urban Centers more walkable over time.
Encourage plazas and other open spaces to provide an inviting atmosphere for pedestrians and support a diversity of uses.
Ensure opportunities for regional shopping destinations, entertainment activities, and employment centers.
Create 18-hour Urban Centers that generate activity throughout the day and evening.
Prioritize transit investment to provide access to and within Urban Centers, potentially spurring additional private investment and transit-oriented development.
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for development form.
See Housing Goal 9.3 for policies related to housing development.

policies.policyDescription: Employment Centers: Create Centers that prioritize employment opportunities and foster synergy among businesses. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Prioritize office and commercial employment in areas with good access via automobile and transit.
Prioritize industrial employment in areas with good connectivity for freight routes.
Until 80 percent of the available land in Employment Centers has developed with uses associated with employment opportunities, discourage residential uses on the ground floor.
After employment has been established on 80 percent of the available land, encourage mixed-use development to introduce high-density residential uses that bring housing to jobs.
Allow Employment Centers to develop as auto-oriented areas.
Provide safe and convenient pedestrian and bicycle access to and mobility within Employment Centers.
Provide good transit access to Employment Centers and connect transit stops/stations to businesses via pedestrian walkways and bikeways.

policies.policyDescription: Activity Centers: Foster mixed-use centers of activity with a range of services and amenities that support healthy lifestyles and meet the needs of nearby residents and businesses. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Incorporate a compatible mix of commercial and residential uses with a range of higher-density housing types.
Provide neighborhood-oriented commercial, retail, institutional, and public services.
Encourage gathering spaces for festivals, markets, and street fairs.
Ensure that Activity Centers are pedestrian-friendly and provide convenient pedestrian connections to nearby residential areas.
Provide good connectivity via bicycle between Activity Centers and nearby residential areas and multi-use trails.
See Urban Design chapter for discussion of neighborhood-scale development and pedestrian-friendly design.

policies.policyDescription: Village Centers: Capture higher-intensity growth within Village Centers as places with a mix of uses that serve the needs of residents in order to protect rural lifestyles and surrounding land use patterns. [BC]

subPolicies.text: Encourage a variety of shopping opportunities.
Provide gathering spaces for festivals, markets, and street fairs.
Focus on tourism in scenic and historic parts of the county in Village Centers.
Allow a range of housing types and densities.

policies.policyDescription: Premium Transit Corridors: Foster corridors that prioritize high-capacity, high-frequency transit service, with mixed-use, transit-oriented development within walking distance of transit stations. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Encourage higher-density residential developments within � mile of identified transit station locations.
Minimize negative impacts on nearby neighborhoods by providing transitions between development along Transit Corridors and abutting single-family residential areas.
Encourage active public spaces and plazas within 660 feet of identified transit station locations and balconies and decks overlooking transit station areas.
Apply Premium Transit policies after station locations have been identified and project funding has been allocated.
See Transportation Policy 6.1.4 for Premium Transit Corridors.
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for development form.

policies.policyDescription: Main Streets: Promote Main Streets that are lively, highly walkable streets lined with neighborhood-oriented businesses. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Prioritize street and walkway improvements, such as street trees, landscaping, lighting, wayfinding, and wide sidewalks, to create safe and comfortable pedestrian environments.
Minimize negative impacts on nearby neighborhoods by providing transitions between Main Street development and abutting single-family residential areas.
Ensure that Main Streets are well-served by multi-modal transportation.

Follow Main Street policy guidance for private property and follow other Corridor designation policies for decisions related to the public right-of-way when a corridor has both the Main Street and another Corridor designation.
See Urban Design chapter for discussion of building and parking orientation.

policies.policyDescription: Major Transit Corridors: Foster corridors that prioritize high-frequency transit service with pedestrian-oriented development. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Encourage higher-density residential developments within � mile of transit stops or stations.
Minimize negative impacts on nearby neighborhoods by providing transitions between development along Transit Corridors and abutting single-family residential areas.
Encourage mixed-use development in Centers and near intersections.
See Transportation Policy 6.1.6 for Major Transit Corridors.
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for development form.

policies.policyDescription: Multi-Modal Corridors: Design safe Multi-Modal Corridors that balance the competing needs of multiple modes of travel and become more mixed-use and pedestrian-oriented over time. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Encourage the redevelopment of aging auto-oriented commercial strip development to a more mixed-use, pedestrian-oriented environment.
Prioritize improvements that increase pedestrian safety and convenience and make bicycle and transit options more viable.
Encourage lower auto traffic speeds and narrower traffic lanes to accommodate other modes of travel in more intense and active areas along the corridor.
See also Transportation Policy 6.1.7 and Goal 6.2 for multi-modal policies.
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for development form.

policies.policyDescription: Commuter Corridors: Allow auto-oriented development along Commuter Corridors that are higher-speed and higher-traffic volume routes for people going across town, often as limited-access roadways. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Allow auto-oriented, single-use development, such as strip retail, large retail facilities, and business and institutional campuses along Commuter Corridors.
Buffer residential land uses adjacent to Commuter Corridors.
Support traffic flow by limiting new curb cuts, encouraging shared access of driveways and business access roads, or providing access from perpendicular local roads.
See Transportation Policy 6.1.8 for Commuter Corridors
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for development form.

goalDetails.detail: Foster communities where residents can live, work, learn, shop, and play together.

policies.policyDescription: Land Uses: Create healthy, sustainable, and distinct communities with a mix of uses that are conveniently accessible from surrounding neighborhoods. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Encourage development and redevelopment that brings goods, services, and amenities within walking and biking distance of neighborhoods and promotes good access for all residents. [ABC]
Encourage development that offers choice in transportation, work areas, and lifestyles. [ABC]
Maintain the characteristics of distinct communities through zoning and design standards that are consistent with long-established residential development patterns. [ABC]
Encourage development that broadens housing options to meet a range of incomes and lifestyles. [ABC]
Encourage higher density housing as an appropriate use in the following situations: [ABC]

subLevels.description: Within designated Centers and Corridors;
In areas with good street connectivity and convenient access to transit;
In areas where a mixed density pattern is already established by zoning or use, where it is compatible with existing area land uses, and where adequate infrastructure is or will be available;
In areas now predominantly zoned single-family only where it comprises a complete block face and faces onto similar or higher density development;
In areas where a transition is needed between single-family homes and much more intensive development.
In Activity Centers with development to serve adjacent neighborhoods with an emphasis on pedestrian and bicycle connections to nearby residential areas;
In larger area-wide shopping centers located near intersections of arterial streets and provided with access via transit;
Next to another shopping center at an intersection only when safe pedestrian crossings are provided to encourage shoppers to �park once� and walk to multiple stores; and
In contiguous storefronts along streets in established neighborhoods and Main Streets.

subPolicies.text: Locate quality commercial development and redevelopment in existing commercial zones and designated Centers and Corridors as follows: [ABC]
Encourage infill development that adds complementary uses and is compatible in form and scale to the immediately surrounding development. [ABC]
Discourage mineral extraction in highly scenic or prime recreational, agricultural, or residential areas. [ABC]
Discourage zone changes to commercial, industrial, or office uses outside of Centers and Corridors. [ABC]
Discourage zone changes to detached single-family residential uses on the West Side. [ABC]
Discourage zone changes to more intense land uses within federally-defined accident potential zones located at the ends of runways, in conjunction with review and comment from KAFB. [ABC]
Discourage zone changes to single land uses on sites larger than ten acres. [A]
Encourage more productive use of vacant lots and under-utilized lots, including surface parking. [A]
See Community Identity Goal 4.1 for policies to preserve distinct communities.
See Community Identity Goal 4.3 for policies about community character in each Community Planning Area.
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for policies on development form.
See Urban Design Goal 7.3 for policies on design elements that reinforce community identity.
See Heritage Conservation chapter for protecting historic areas and scenic views.

policies.policyDescription: Planned Communities: Include Centers and Corridors in master planned communities and follow the Planned Communities Criteria (PCC) as adopted by the City and County. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Acquire land within planned communities to preserve required Open Space. [ABC]
Encourage a mix of single-family and townhouse lots with a range of sizes to support choice in housing and to meet the needs of all income levels. [ABC]
Ensure coordination and phasing of Planned Communities in Rural and Reserve Areas that achieve the policies for each Development Area. [BC]

Encourage appropriate jobs-housing mix by requiring that balanced employment be developed and established before additional residential development can be approved within a Planned Community at the end of a development phase. [BC]
See Policy 5.5.2 below for policies on Planned Communities in Reserve Areas.
See Policy 5.5.3 below for policies on Planned Communities in Rural Areas.

goalDetails.detail: Promote development patterns that maximize the utility of existing infrastructure and public facilities and the efficient use of land to support the public good.

policies.policyDescription: Infill Development: Support additional growth in areas with existing infrastructure and public facilities. [ABC]
Leapfrog Development: Discourage growth in areas without existing infrastructure and public facilities. [ABC]
Compact Development: Encourage development that clusters buildings and uses in order to provide landscaped open space and/or plazas and courtyards. [ABC]

Conservation Development: Encourage conservation development to promote private open space and preserve natural landscape, agricultural lands, and other features of the natural environment to encourage development that is sensitive to the open, natural character of the area and the geological and cultural conditions. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Use conservation easements to limit construction and ecologically harmful activities to provide a buffer to ecologically sensitive areas. [A]

Encourage use of development envelopes to define areas in which buildings (including accessory structures), landscaping, construction activity, walls and fences, and recreational activities are permitted. [A]

Use cluster development to concentrate buildings on a portion of the site, in particular near floodplains or other natural features, to allow the remaining land to be used for recreation, open space, agriculture, or preservation of sensitive land areas. [A]
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.5.3 for prioritizing public investment to direct growth.

policies.policyDescription: School Capacity: Discourage zone changes from non-residential to residential or mixed-use zones when affected public schools have insufficient capacity to support the anticipated increase of students based on proposed dwelling units. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Housing Goal 9.1 for policies that encourage mixed-income neighborhoods.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.4.3 for improved coordination with APS.

policies.policyDescription: Reassembly and Replatting: Encourage property owner coordination to reassemble areas prematurely subdivided or platted that have inadequate right-of-way or drainage before infrastructure and services are extended. [ABC]

Locally Unwanted Land Uses: Ensure that land uses that are objectionable to immediate neighbors but may be useful to society are located carefully and equitably to ensure that social assets are distributed evenly and social responsibilities are borne fairly across the Albuquerque area. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Minimize the impacts of locally unwanted land uses on surrounding areas through policies, regulations, and enforcement.
Ensure appropriate setbacks, buffers, and/or design standards to minimize offsite impacts.
See Policy 5.2.1 above for desired land uses to support complete communities.
See Community Identity Goal 4.1 for policies to preserve distinct communities.
See Community Identity Policy 4.2.1 for the Community Planning Area assessment process.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.3.9 about behavioral health services.
See Resilience & Sustainability Goal 13.5 for protecting community health and welfare.

policies.policyDescription: Solar Protections: Protect solar access to encourage solar energy collection and healthy living conditions. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Encourage platting and street layout that facilitates solar access.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.1.6 related to energy systems.
See Resilience & Sustainability Policy 13.4.3 for energy conservation policies.

goalDetails.detail: Balance jobs and housing by encouraging residential growth near employment across the region and prioritizing job growth west of the Rio Grande.

policies.policyDescription: Housing near Jobs: Allow higher-density housing and discourage single-family housing near areas with concentrated employment. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Prioritize higher-density housing where services and infrastructure are available.
Prioritize mixed-use development near where substantial employment exists in Employment Centers.
See Housing Goal 9.1 for policies about housing supply and affordability.

policies.policyDescription: West Side Jobs: Foster employment opportunities on the West Side. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Ensure adequate capacity of land zoned for commercial, office, and industrial uses west of the Rio Grande to support additional job growth.
Prioritize employment opportunities within Centers.
Prioritize incentives and support for employers providing base employment on the West Side.
Promote the clustering of employment opportunities within business parks or industrial parks served by transit.
See also Policy 5.1.5 above for policies about Employment Centers.
See Economic Development Goal 8.1 for policies about planning for and targeting employment growth.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 13.1.2 on water and wastewater infrastructure.

goalDetails.detail: Use Development Areas to foster the distinctness of communities in the unincorporated County by guiding their form, character, and density. [BC]

policies.policyDescription: Community Green Space: Provide visual relief from urbanization and offer opportunities for education, recreation, cultural activities, and conservation of natural resources by setting aside publicly-owned Open Space, parks, trail corridors, and open areas throughout the Comp Plan area as mapped in Figure 5-3. [BC]

subPolicies.text: Maintain existing irrigation systems as Community Green Space to help ensure agricultural lands in rural areas.
See Urban Design policy 7.3.1 for design that preserves natural and cultural features.
See Urban Design policy 7.6.1 for drainage infrastructure design.
See the Parks & Open Space chapter for discussion of community green space and policies to acquire and preserve land.
See Heritage Conservation Goals 11.1 and 11.3 for rural heritage and cultural landscapes.
See also Resiliency & Sustainability Goal 13.4 for policies to preserve natural resources.

policies.policyDescription: Reserve Areas: Allow opportunity for future development of high quality, mixed-use, largely self-sufficient planned communities, bounded by permanent open-space, in appropriate outlying areas, and protect and maintain the non-urban development areas as Rural unless such planned communities are developed. [BC]

subPolicies.text: Use Figure 5-5 to determine where Reserve Area policies apply.
Accommodate a portion of growth in new planned communities in Reserve Areas. Such communities should meet the following guidelines:

subLevels.description: Provide employment, goods, and public services, with at least one type of Center within each planned community as adequate to be substantially self-sufficient.
Ensure that housing quantity, type, price, and location correspond to employment opportunities.

Negotiate service cost sharing between the developer and the local government, with water, sewer, and street systems installed to meet County requirements to ensure that planned communities are not a net expense to local governments.
Provide transit/para-transit service within planned communities and connect with other urban areas.
Separate and distinguish new communities from existing Urban Areas with dedicated Open Space.
Provide a viable and sustainable mix of housing types to be affordable for a range of income levels.
Ensure contiguous acreage for each planned community that meets the above guidelines.
Transfer development rights to local government to ensure the permanency of the pattern.
Calculate density using all land except publicly owned land (whether fee or easement), including Indian Tribal lands.

Conduct a carrying capacity analysis of each planned community area to identify constraints and opportunities presented by environmental, historical, cultural, archaeological, and infrastructure factors.

subPolicies.text: Maintain open space and densities under three dwelling units per acre in areas outside of planned communities and use density transfer (clustering) to accomplish appropriate urban densities within planned communities. Prescribe housing densities, land use mix, open space, infrastructure size and location, and other public services and facilities through Level A and B plans.

Require development within Reserve Areas to take place either in accordance with an approved planned community master plan (up to three dwelling units per acre), or in accordance with the standards applicable to Rural Areas.

Implement the Comp Plan by reviewing planned community master plans per this section and the Planned Communities Criteria (PCC) as adopted by the City and County and denying planned community master plans that fail to demonstrate a sense of place, self-sufficiency, environmental sensitivity, separation from the contiguous Albuquerque urban area by permanent open space, and provision of infrastructure at no net expense to the local government(s).
Require compliance with both the PCC and the Comp Plan policies for Level A, Level B, and Level C plans.
See Policy 5.2.2 above for planned development.
See Policy 5.5.1 above for community green space.
See Policy 5.7.2 and associated actions below for former Comp Plan �Possible Implementation Techniques� that apply to all County Development Areas.
See also Urban Design Policy 7.3.2 for design that reflects community character.

policies.policyDescription: Rural Areas: Maintain the separate identity of Rural Areas as alternatives to urbanization by guiding development compatible with their open character, natural resources, and traditional settlement patterns. [BC]

subPolicies.text: Use Figure 5-5 to determine where Rural Area policies apply.

Retain the rural character of areas designated as Rural Areas on the Development Area map by allowing development consisting primarily of ranches, farms, and single-family homes on large lots, with overall gross densities not exceeding one dwelling unit per acre. Development in these areas should follow these guidelines:

subLevels.description: Allow higher-density development at appropriate locations within Village Centers, rural villages, or planned communities, but retain appropriate overall-area gross density through dedication of open space.
Define Rural Area density patterns through lower rank plans.
Encourage clustered development, setbacks from ditches, and conservation easements to preserve farmland, ditches, and open space.
Each higher density area is to be controlled by a site development plan and is to be located well away from other such higher density areas.
Allow small �rural villages� with compact housing areas - usually no more than 100 dwellings - and very few stores to serve the village.
Follow Reserve Area policies for planned communities within rural villages, but with lower gross density requirements.
In the East Mountain area , allow urban densities, to be determined by lower ranking plans.
Maintain and integrate existing buildings, features, and landscapes determined to be of significant local, State, and/or National interest as viable elements of the community.
Ensure that new rural development is sensitive to existing historic, cultural, and economic patterns.
Encourage small-scale, local, or owner-operated industries which employ few people and may sell products on the same premises as the most desirable commercial or industrial use.
Discourage mineral extraction in highly scenic or prime recreational, agricultural, or residential areas.
Regulate noise and pollution levels and require restoration of land where mineral extraction and industrial development occurs.
Allow neighborhood and/or community-scale commercial centers as appropriate uses in rural areas.

Discourage strip commercial development and, instead, encourage clustered commercial development at major intersections, in Employment Centers, and within designated mountain and valley Village Centers.

Discourage special use permits for commercial and industrial uses outside of Village Centers and limit special use permits for non-residential uses to terms of no more than five years, except for utilities.

subPolicies.text: Ensure that development in Rural Areas is compatible with natural resource capacities, including water availability, acequias, soil capacity, community and regional goals, and includes trail corridors where appropriate. Maintain rural densities where water and sewer service is not available.

subLevels.description: Approve new rural villages and planned communities only if all public infrastructure needed to serve the proposed areas is provided at no cost to the County.

subPolicies.text: Carefully control development in floodplains and valley areas where flood danger, high water table, soils, and air inversions inhibit extensive urbanization.

Maintain, to the extent feasible, land that is suitable for agriculture in agricultural production using a variety of techniques, including conservation easements, acquisition of properties, and agricultural zoning, to discourage non-agricultural development in these areas.
Guide development of inhabited rural settlements of a distinctive historic and cultural character using the following policies:
Control development in the East Mountains and the Valley to prevent environmental deterioration and ensure compatibility with the resource base and natural recreational and scenic assets.
Guide industrial and commercial development in Rural Areas using the following policies:

Extend County public services and facilities to Rural Areas only where: (1 public health and safety are threatened and there is no safe alternative; (2) a planned community is approved and being developed, for which extension of certain services and facilities is economically feasible and environmentally sound; or (3) the extension is part of an adopted policy of metropolitan area service.
See Policy 5.5.1 above for community green space.
See Policies 5.5.4 and 5.5.6 below for requirements for higher density development.
See Policy 5.7.2 and associated actions below for former Comp Plan �Possible Implementation Techniques� that apply to all County Development Areas.
See also Urban Design Policy 7.3.2 for design that reflects community character.
See Heritage Conservation Goal 11.1 for rural and agricultural heritage.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services chapter for additional policies on infrastructure provision.

policies.policyDescription: Semi-Urban Areas: Maintain the character and identity of the Semi-Urban areas that have environmental, social, or cultural conditions limiting urban land uses. [BC]

subPolicies.text: Use Figure 5-5 to determine where Semi-Urban Area policies apply.

Enforce development limitations imposed by topography, acequia easements, soil conditions, groundwater quality, agricultural potential, flood potential, scenic qualities, recreation potential and existing development for Semi-Urban areas, as defined by the Plan map.
Maintain overall gross density up to three dwelling units per acre, or as specified in County Sector Plans.
Include trail corridors, where appropriate in Semi-Urban areas.
Encourage development that is compatible with economic policies and historical and socio-cultural values.
Maintain and integrate existing and new buildings and spaces of local significance into the community.
Encourage agricultural uses, particularly where access to acequias and other forms of irrigation is available.
Encourage cluster housing to preserve farmland and open space and setbacks from ditches and conservation easements to preserve farmland and ditches.
Guide industrial and commercial development using the following policies in Semi-Urban areas:

subLevels.description: Encourage neighborhood- and community-scale commercial centers as appropriate;
Discourage strip commercial development in favor of clustered commercial development in Village Centers or along designated corridors;
Create mixed-use areas that protect residential uses in the area, while offering a variety of local employment opportunities;
Discourage mineral extraction in highly scenic or prime recreational, agricultural, or residential areas;

Discourage special use permits for commercial and industrial uses outside of Village Centers or along designated corridors. Where non-residential permits are requested and deemed appropriate, they should be limited to terms of no more than 5 years, except utilities.
Determine boundaries for each area plan based upon design character, social and cultural identity, and visual and environmental features.

Determine content of each area plan based upon needs analysis, including but not limited to characteristics, conditions, trends and opportunities in land use, the built and visual environment, and social and economic environment.
Determine development potential of each plan area in keeping with density objectives of the Comp Plan.
Determine Center appropriateness and character for each area in coordination with the area-wide Centers implementation planning, as developed in future planning efforts.
Within designated Centers and Corridors;
In areas with excellent access to the major street network and transit;
In areas where a mixed density pattern is already established by zoning or use, where it is compatible with existing area land uses, and where adequate infrastructure is or will be available;
In areas now predominantly zoned single-family only where it comprises a complete block face and faces onto similar or higher density development: up to 10 dwelling units per net acre;

In areas where a transition is needed between single-family homes and much more intensive development: densities will vary up to 30 dwelling units per net acre according to the intensity of development in adjacent areas.
In small neighborhood-oriented centers with parking located in the back and side and pedestrian and bicycle connections to nearby residential areas;
In larger area-wide shopping centers located at intersections of arterial streets and with access via transit;
More than one shopping center should be allowed at an intersection only when safe pedestrian crossings are provided to encourage �park once and walk� opportunities;
In free-standing retailing and contiguous storefronts along streets in older neighborhoods.

subPolicies.text: See Policy 5.7.2 and associated actions below for former Comp Plan �Possible Implementation Techniques� that apply to all County Development Areas.
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for policies on designing rights of way.
See Heritage Conservation Goal 11.1 for rural and agricultural heritage.
See Heritage Conservation Goal 11.3 for cultural landscapes and view protection.
Use Figure 5-5 to determine where Developing and Established Urban Area policies apply.

Allow a full range of urban land uses resulting in an overall gross density up to five dwelling units per acre within Developing and Established Urban Areas as shown by the Plan map, with higher densities specified in area or sector plans.
Develop and adopt sector development plans stating density patterns.

Develop and enforce special requirements for low-density holding zones to allow for sector planning, special design treatments, and phasing of infrastructure in keeping with capital investment priorities in Developing Urban Areas.

Where needed to guide more detailed planning, combine major portions of the Established and Developing Urban Areas and adjacent Plan map areas into districts that correspond to Community Planning Areas, using the following process:

Encourage the location, intensity, and design of new development to respect existing neighborhood values, natural environmental conditions and carrying capacities, scenic resources, acequia easements, and resources of other social, cultural, and recreational concern.

Accommodate new growth through infill and compact development in areas where vacant land is contiguous to existing or programmed urban facilities and services and where the integrity of existing neighborhoods can be ensured.
Encourage clustering of homes to provide larger shared open areas and houses oriented towards walkways or bikeways.
Ensure that development is carefully designed to conform to topographical features and include trail corridors and dedicated Open Space where appropriate.
Encourage higher density housing as an appropriate use in the following situations:
Ensure that employment and service uses are located to complement residential areas and are sited to minimize adverse effects of noise, lighting, pollution, and traffic on residential environments.
Locate new commercial development in existing commercially zoned areas and designated Centers and Corridors as follows:

Encourage quality and innovation in design that is appropriate to the Plan area in all new development, including solar orientation and panels, cisterns and water harvesting, xeriscaping, adobe and rammed earth construction, and other green technologies.
Encourage urban and site design that maintains and enhances unique vistas and improves the quality of the visual environment.
Reassemble or sector plan areas prematurely subdivided with problematic ownership, platting, inadequate right-of-way, or drainage before service extension is assured.
Continue to redevelop and rehabilitate older neighborhoods in the Established Urban Area.
Develop and use cost-effective redevelopment techniques, including Metropolitan Redevelopment Areas, Tax Increment Financing Districts, Main Street Districts, and others.
See Policy 5.7.2 and associated actions below for former Comp Plan �Possible Implementation Techniques� that apply to all County Development Areas.
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for policies on designing rights of way.
See Heritage Conservation Goal 11.3 for cultural landscapes and view protection.

goalDetails.detail: Encourage and direct growth to Areas of Change where it is expected and desired and ensure that development in and near Areas of Consistency reinforces the character and intensity of the surrounding area.

policies.policyDescription: Community Green Space: Provide visual relief from urbanization and offer opportunities for education, recreation, cultural activities, and conservation of natural resources by setting aside publicly-owned Open Space, parks, trail corridors, and open areas throughout the Comp Plan area as mapped in Figure 5-3. [A]

subPolicies.text: Maintain existing irrigation systems as Community Green Space to help ensure agricultural lands in rural areas.
See Urban Design policy 7.3.1 for design that preserves natural and cultural features.
See Urban Design policy 7.6.1 for drainage infrastructure design.
See Parks & Open Space chapter Goals 10.1 and 10.3 for open space facilities, access, acquisition, and protection.
See Heritage Conservation Goals 11.1 and 11.3 for rural heritage and cultural landscapes.
See also Resiliency & Sustainability Goal 13.4 for policies to preserve natural resources.

policies.policyDescription: Areas of Change: Direct growth and more intense development to Centers, Corridors, industrial and business parks, and Metropolitan Redevelopment Areas where change is encouraged. [A]

subPolicies.text: Use Figure 5-6 created according to the methodology described in Section 5.1.2.5, to determine where Areas of Change policies apply.
Encourage development that expands employment opportunities.
Foster a range of housing options at various densities according to each Center or Corridor type.
Encourage higher-density housing and mixed-use development as appropriate land uses that support transit and commercial and retail uses.
Encourage job creation in business and industrial parks, near freight routes, and where adequate transitions and buffers can be provided to protect abutting residential uses.
Minimize potential negative impacts of development on existing residential uses with respect to noise, stormwater runoff, contaminants, lighting, air quality, and traffic.
Encourage development where adequate infrastructure and community services exist.
Encourage development in areas with a highly connected street grid and frequent transit service.
Discourage zone changes from industrial uses to either mixed-use or residential zones.
See Goal 5.1 above for policies on relevant Centers and/or Corridors.
See Goal 5.4 above for policies on improving the jobs-housing balance and targeting employment opportunities.
See Policy 5.6.4 below on appropriate transitions where Areas of Change abut Areas of Consistency.

policies.policyDescription: Areas of Consistency: Protect and enhance the character of existing single-family neighborhoods, areas outside of Centers and Corridors, parks, and Major Public Open Space. [A]

subPolicies.text: Use Figure 5-6 created according to the methodology described in Section 5.1.2.5, to determine where Areas of Consistency policies apply.
Ensure that development reinforces the scale, intensity, and setbacks of the immediately surrounding context.
Carefully consider zone changes from residential to non-residential zones in terms of scale, impact on land use compatibility with abutting properties, and context.
In areas with predominantly single-family residential uses, support zone changes that help align the appropriate zone with existing land uses.

In areas with predominantly non-residential uses, carefully consider zone changes from non-residential to mixed-use or residential zones for potential impact on land use compatibility with abutting properties, employment opportunities, and historic development patterns.

Limit the location of higher-density housing and mixed-use development to areas within � mile of transit stations and within 660 feet of arterials and Corridors as an appropriate transition to single-family neighborhoods.
Provide stepbacks and/or setbacks to protect solar access and privacy on abutting single-family residential properties.
See Policy 5.6.4 below on appropriate transitions where Areas of Consistency abut Areas of Change.
See Community Identity Policy 4.1.4 to preserve and enhance distinct communities
See Heritage Conservation Goal 11.3 for policies on appropriate development near to parks and open space areas.

policies.policyDescription: Appropriate Transitions: Provide transitions in Areas of Change for development abutting Areas of Consistency through adequate setbacks, buffering, and limits on building height and massing. [A]

subPolicies.text: Provide appropriate transitions between uses of different intensity or density and between non-residential uses and single-family neighborhoods to protect the character and integrity of existing residential areas.
Minimize development�s negative effects on individuals and neighborhoods with respect to noise, lighting, air pollution, and traffic.
See Policy 5.3.7 above for addressing objectionable land uses.

goalDetails.detail: Employ procedures and processes to effectively and equitably implement the Comp Plan.

policies.policyDescription: Coordinated Public Investment: Prioritize public investments and program funding by the City and County to be consistent with and to implement Comp Plan land use goals and policies. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Prioritize investment in Centers and Corridors. [ABC]
Prioritize investment in Areas of Change with existing infrastructure that needs to be upgraded. [A]
See Community Identity Policy 4.1.4 for partnerships and investment in existing neighborhoods.
See Transportation Policy 6.7.1 for public investment and partnerships to improve the transportation system.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Goal 12.5 for policies related to public investment in infrastructure, facilities, and services.

policies.policyDescription: Regulatory Alignment: Update regulatory frameworks to support desired growth, high quality development, economic development, housing, a variety of transportation modes, and quality of life priorities. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Create pathways for economic growth and support the business ecosystem by providing clear development codes and processes. [ABC]
Limit the use of Special Use Permits (SU-Permits) in the County through the following mechanisms: [BC]

subLevels.description: Inventory and map all SU-permits by use.
Specify and limit the types of uses that may be considered under SU-permits to uses not available in another zoning category.
Evaluate zones to expand list of permissible uses.
Consolidate zoning for parcels split between SU-permits and by-right zones.
Allow home business permits when appropriate in place of SU-permits.
Use conditional use permits in place of SU-permits when practical.

subPolicies.text: Avoid the use of SU-1 as a tool to negotiate design or use standards between stakeholders and limit its application to uses specified in the SU-1 zone. [A]

policies.policyDescription: Updated Centers and Corridors: Add, update, or delete Centers and Corridors as needed to shape the built environment in a manner consistent with the Comp Plan Vision for the future. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Identify potential changes to Centers and Corridors through community outreach and planning efforts, including Sector Developments Plans in the County and the Community Planning Area assessment process in the city.
Analyze potential updates to Centers or Corridors in collaboration with City and County departments and other agencies.
Propose changes to Centers or Corridors as amendments to the Comp Plan and Vision Map for adoption by the City and County.

policies.policyDescription: Streamlined Development: Encourage efficiencies in the development review process. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Encourage and facilitate meetings between developers and residents to identify and address issues prior to the official submittal of projects for approval.
Encourage and facilitate pre-application review by staff and relevant departments/agencies to facilitate coordinated reviews and early identification and resolution of issues.
Provide streamlined approval processes for projects that meet the intent of the Comp Plan.
Provide by-right approval processes for projects that meet regulatory standards.
See Economic Development Goal 8.1 for policies to create places that support business development and success.

policies.policyDescription: Public Engagement: Provide regular opportunities for residents and stakeholders to better understand and engage in the planning and development process. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Coordinate with developers and lenders to remove obstacles and identify effective incentives for desired development. [A]
See Community Identity Goals 4.2 and 4.3 for the CPA assessment process and City Leaders to engage the public on a regular basis.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policies 12.5.5 and 12.5.6 for policies about staff capacity and public input in public resource allocation.
See Appendix E for a description of how the City will plan with communities in the future through the CPA assessment process and regular City Leaders programs.

goalDetails.detail: Plan, develop, operate, and maintain a transportation system to support the planned character of existing and future land uses.

policies.policyDescription: Matching Land Use: When designing and improving streets, prioritize transportation-related accommodations and amenities to match the desired development context (e.g. urban, suburban, or rural) and/or the intended intensity of land uses. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: In urban areas, accommodate pedestrians as the highest priority travel mode, provide frequent transit service, limit or prohibit curb cuts, lower auto travel speeds, and accept auto congestion. [ABC]

On major streets in suburban areas, prioritize auto through-put, allow more frequent curb cuts, design for higher auto travel speeds, emphasize pedestrian safety at intersections, and provide less frequent transit service geared more toward long-distance commuting. [ABC]
On local streets, design for the slowest auto travel speeds to best accommodate pedestrians and cyclists and to protect livability and safety of established residential neighborhoods. [ABC]

In rural areas, plan streets based on functional classification for a variety of travel conditions, balancing access to destinations with auto through-put, allowing more frequent curb cuts and moderate auto travel speeds, emphasizing pedestrian safety at intersections, and provide less frequent transit service geared more toward long-distance commuting. [BC]
See Policies 6.1.4-6.1.9 below for individual Corridors.
See Goal 6.2 below for multi-modal considerations.
See Land Use Policies 5.1.3-5.1.12 for land use priorities in Centers and along Corridors.
See Urban Design chapter Section 7.1.2.1 for discussion of development context.
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for hierarchy of Centers and Corridors policy application and for development form guidance.

policies.policyDescription: Transit-Oriented Development: Prioritize transit-supportive density, uses, and building design along Transit Corridors. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Identify transit-oriented development opportunities when planning transit service on Major Transit and Premium Transit Corridors. [A]
Prioritize pedestrian amenities and a higher level of connectivity within 660 feet of transit stations. [A]
Design streets to best accommodate transit vehicles and pedestrians, with bicycle accommodation focused on direct connections to the stations/stops, rather than along the travel way. [A]
See Policy 6.2.7 below for transit network policies.
See Land Use Goal 5.1 for policies about development in Centers and along Corridors.

policies.policyDescription: Auto Demand: Reduce the need for automobile travel by increasing mixed-use development, infill development within Centers, and travel demand management (TDM) programs. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Community Identity Goal 4.2 for the CPA assessment process.
See Land Use Goals 5.1 and 5.2 for desired growth patterns and land uses.
See also Urban Design Goal 7.1 for streetscape design and development form in Centers and along Corridors.

policies.policyDescription: Premium Transit Corridors: Prioritize transit vehicles within the travel way and transit users in street design and improvements, incorporating pedestrian amenities, such as bulb-outs, pedestrian-activated signals, and refuge medians at intersections and near transit stations. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Within Centers or within 660 feet of transit stations, follow policy objectives in the �Centers & Stations� column in Table 6-3.
For the remainder of the Corridor, follow policy objectives related to the relevant underlying designation in Policies 6.1.4-6.1.9.
For Corridors without an underlying designation, follow policy objectives in the �Other� column in Table 6-3.
See Land Use Goal 5.1 for policies about Centers & Corridors.
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for policy hierarchy for Centers & Corridors and Development Form and Priority Street Elements Matrices.

policies.policyDescription: Main Street Corridors: Prioritize pedestrians in street design and improvements, emphasizing safety, accommodation, and amenities by slowing auto traffic, providing on-street parking, limiting or prohibiting curb cuts, and requiring primary auto access to parking lots to be provided from intersecting, auto-oriented streets. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Design streets within Main Street Corridors according to Table 6-4.
See Land Use Goal 5.1.9 for additional policies on Main Streets.
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for policy hierarchy for Centers & Corridors and Development Form and Priority Street Elements Matrices.

policies.policyDescription: Major Transit Corridors: Prioritize transit users in street design and improvements, encouraging pedestrian amenities, such as bulb-outs, pedestrian-activated signals, and refuge medians at intersections and near transit stops and stations. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Explore multi-modal and operational opportunities to manage congestion on river crossings, such as limited directional traffic during peak hours.
Within Centers or within 660 feet of transit stations, follow policy objectives in the �Centers & Stations� column in Table 6-5.
For the remainder of the Corridor, follow policy objectives in the �Other� column in Table 6-5.
See Land Use Policy 5.1.10 for additional policies on Major Transit Corridors.
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for policy hierarchy for Centers & Corridors and Development Form and Priority Street Elements Matrices.

policies.policyDescription: Multi-Modal Corridors: Balance the competing needs of pedestrians, bicyclists, autos, and transit in street design and improvements by slowing auto traffic, minimizing curb cuts, and encouraging primary auto access to parking lots to be provided from intersecting streets. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Accommodate all users, providing safety and mobility for pedestrians, bicyclists, and people with physical disabilities.
Where insufficient right-of-way exists within the Corridor, provide enhanced facilities for bicyclists and pedestrians along parallel streets.
Within Centers or within 660 feet of transit stations, follow policy objectives in the �Centers & Stations� column in Table 6-6.
For the remainder of the Corridor, follow policy objectives in the �Other� column in Table 6-6.
See Land Use Policy 5.1.11 for additional policies on Multi-Modal Corridors.
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for policy hierarchy for Centers & Corridors and Development Form and Priority Street Elements Matrices.

policies.policyDescription: Commuter Corridors: Prioritize automobile travel in street design and improvements by allowing higher traffic speeds, managing access for autos and pedestrians, and improving safety for pedestrians at signalized intersections. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Within Centers or within 660 feet of transit stations, follow policy objectives in the �Centers & Stations� column in Table 6-7.
For the remainder of the Corridor, follow policy objectives in the �Other� column in Table 6-7.
See Land Use Policy 5.1.12 for additional policies on Commuter Corridors.
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for policy hierarchy for Centers & Corridors and Development Form and Priority Street Elements Matrices.

policies.policyDescription: Other Arterials: Coordinate transportation planning at the regional level for arterials not designated as corridors in the Comp Plan. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Plan land adjacent to arterial streets to minimize harmful effects of traffic on nearby roadways and development.
Reduce the frequency of curb cuts along principal and minor arterials.
Within Centers or within 660 feet of transit stations, follow policy objectives in the �Centers & Stations� column in Table 6-8.
For the remainder of arterial streets, follow policy objectives in the �Other� column in Table 6-8.
See also Goal 6.2 below for multi-modal system policies.
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for policy hierarchy for Centers & Corridors and Development Form and Priority Street Elements Matrices.

goalDetails.detail: Encourage walking, biking, and transit, especially at peak-hour commuting times, to enhance access and mobility for people of all ages and abilities.

policies.policyDescription: Complete Networks: Design and build a complete, well-connected network of streets and trails that offer multiple efficient and safe transportation choices for commuting and daily needs. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Prioritize investment in facilities for walking, biking, and transit.
Provide safe and efficient transfers between transportation modes.

Improve connectivity to provide route options and decrease distance to services for all users, particularly vulnerable populations, by reinforcing the street grid, providing pedestrian access points through subdivisions, and discouraging right-of-way vacations, etc.
Discourage dead ends in order to improve pedestrian and bicycle access to transit and other destinations and reduce congestion by dispersing automobile traffic.
Improve connections between transit stops and where people live and work through increasing active transportation options.
Improve safe trails and biking infrastructure and ensure that these improvements are equitably distributed to increase access to and enjoyment of open space by all residents.

policies.policyDescription: Complete Streets: Incorporate Complete Streets concepts and policies into the development, retrofit, and rehabilitation of all transportation infrastructure at all phases, including planning, scoping, design, implementation, and performance monitoring. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Use best practices for multi-modal design.
Minimize conflicts between vehicular traffic and pedestrians and cyclists and incorporate traffic calming and safety measures for pedestrians and bicyclists.

Apply best practices and national design guidance from sources such as the ITE Manual for Context Sensitive Solutions in Designing Major Urban Thoroughfares for Walkable Communities, NACTO Urban Street Design Guide, AASHTO Guide for the Planning, Design, and Operation of Pedestrian Facilities, NACTO Urban Bikeway Design Guide, and AASHTO Guide for the Development of Bicycle Facilities.
See Street Design guidance in Policies 6.1.4-6.1.9 above.
See also Urban Design Goals 7.1 and 7.1 for streetscape and pedestrian-accessible design.

policies.policyDescription: Pedestrian & Bicycle Connectivity: Provide direct pedestrian and bicycle access to and circulation within Centers, commercial properties, community facilities, and residential neighborhoods. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Design streets, streetscapes, and sidewalks to enhance pedestrian and bicyclist mobility for commuting, recreation, and activities of daily living. [ABC]
Preserve and maintain pedestrian, biking, and equestrian opportunities on neighborhood streets, in alleys, and along acequias. [ABC]
Provide comfortable, barrier-free, direct pedestrian and bicycle routes to Transit Centers, transit stations, and transit stops. [ABC]
Ensure pedestrian connections to private open space and civic spaces in Centers. [ABC]
Design subdivisions to provide multiple vehicular and pedestrian access points. [ABC]
Design pedestrian and bicycle circulation systems within private developments to fit the character of the site and minimize conflicts with vehicular traffic. [A]
Invest in City-funded sidewalk improvements for safety and accessibility for all users, and especially people with limited mobility.
See Policy 6.2.1 above for complete networks.
See Policy 6.2.4 Pedestrian Network below.
See Policy 6.2.5 Bicycle Network below.
See Policy 6.5.2 below for ADA compliance.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.4.2 for ADA compliance.

policies.policyDescription: Pedestrian Network: Prioritize pedestrian travel, safety, and amenities above all other transportation modes on Main Street Corridors and streets within Downtown, Urban Centers, and Activity Centers. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Develop and maintain a safe, convenient, and visually pleasing pedestrian environment, ensuring adequate facilities for all users, especially children, senior citizens, and people with disabilities. [ABC]
Encourage small-scale uses, pedestrian-oriented site layout and design, and safe crossings at intersections and transit stops, stations, park and ride locations, and transit centers. [ABC]

Prioritize pedestrian level of service, accommodations, and amenities over auto traffic speeds at transit stations, within Centers, and at crossings of limited-access and high-traffic arterials. [ABC]
Implement and update the County�s Pedestrian and Bicycle Safety Action Plan: http://www.bernco.gov/public-works/pedestrian-bicycle-safety-study-action-plan.aspx. [BC]
See Policy 6.5.2 below for ADA compliance.
See Land Use Goal 5.1 for Centers and Corridors policies.
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for the Priority Street Elements Matrix.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.4.2 for ADA compliance.

policies.policyDescription: Bicycle Network: Promote an area-wide bicycle and trail network for transportation and recreation that emphasizes connections among Centers and safe crossings at intersections. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Create a comprehensive, safe, and convenient network of bike routes, lanes, and trails, by incorporating bicycle facilities into all future transportation planning. [ABC]

Prioritize safe and convenient connections for bicyclists among Centers, existing parks, trails, and Open Space that will result in a continuous and interconnected system of bikeways and trails. [ABC]
Require bike facilities and trails as part of private developments to implement Rank 2 Bikeways Facilities Plans. [ABC]

Where bikeways and trails are planned along streets with high traffic speeds or volumes, including Commuter and Multi-Modal Corridors, provide buffered bike lanes and/or off-street trails to allow the greatest separation between cyclists and automobiles. [ABC]
Prioritize trail and bikeway improvements in the County�s Pedestrian and Bicycle Safety Action Plan and the City�s Rank 2 Bikeways & Trails Facility Plan. [ABC]
Design and develop bicycle facilities to meet safety considerations as provided in the LRTS Guide, ITE, NACTO, and/or AASHTO standards. [ABC]
Incorporate the best and most innovative facilities that can be provided within right-of-way, project, and budget constraints. [A]
Improve bicycle parking options in commercial areas, civic spaces, and recreation destinations. [A]
See Policy 6.2.3 above for connectivity.
See Policy 6.7.2 below for coordination of the regional bicycle and trail network.
See Land Use Goal 5.1 for Centers & Corridors policies.
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for the Priority Street Elements Matrix.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.4.5 for Facility Plans.

policies.policyDescription: Equestrian Network: Follow guidance on equestrian facilities in the Bikeways & Trails Facility Plan; the Bicyclist and Pedestrian Safety Action Plan; and the Parks, Recreation, and Open Space Plan. [ABC]
Transit Network: Prioritize transit travel and pedestrian safety, especially near transit stops and stations and intersections. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Locate Park and Ride facilities to serve areas with high auto congestion and design facilities to maximize auto access and minimize adverse impacts on single-family residential areas. [ABC]

Design and invest in an efficient and reliable transit system that serves existing development and provides premium service in Centers and along Transit Corridors, with the highest level of service Downtown and in Urban Centers. [A]

Make transit access easy, comfortable, convenient, safe, and more viable by providing direct routes, increasing frequency, achieving acceptable system-wide travel speeds, and employing new technology. [A]

Provide the highest level of transit service, dedicated transit lanes, and amenities on corridors with transit-supportive land uses, intensities, and design; where ridership is expected to be greatest; or where auto congestion signals the greatest need for additional transportation options. [A]

Employ strategies and technology, such as signal preference and preboarding fare systems, to improve travel times for transit on Premium and Major Transit Corridors, particularly for peak-hour travel. [A]
Locate transit stations to maximize the number of residences and businesses within �-mile to expand and sustain transit ridership. [A]
