Design transit centers and stations to provide good lighting, shade, seating, information and wayfinding, and bicycle storage. [A]
Improve safety of buses and bus stops for vulnerable populations (e.g., women and children, people with disabilities, other persons) by incorporating safe design strategies.
See Policy 6.1.2 above for transit-oriented development.
See Policy 6.2.3 above for pedestrian and bicycle connections to transit.
See Policy 6.2.4 above for the pedestrian network.
See Goal 6.3 below for safe transportation systems.
See Policy 6.7.2 below for policies on regional transit coordination.
See Land Use Goals 5.1 and 5.2 for policies to encourage high-density residential development, mixed-income residential, and a mix of employment and services near transit service.
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for policies related to pedestrian and transit-supportive streetscape design.

policies.policyDescription: Auto Network: Prioritize automobile travel on Commuter Corridors and balance it with other travel modes on other streets. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Provide continuous, safe, and convenient vehicular circulation to achieve and maintain smooth traffic flow at steady, moderate speeds. [ABC]

On Commuter Corridors and other auto-oriented arterials, provide convenient access to auto-oriented uses, minimize conflicts with pedestrians and cyclists, and provide safe and convenient pedestrian crossings. [ABC]
On Commuter Corridors within Centers, prioritize the pedestrian network. [ABC]
On transit and pedestrian-oriented arterials, balance auto traffic flow with other travel modes to achieve multi-modal, mixed-use, environments. [ABC]
Coordinate vehicle circulation throughout development sites, clearly define access points, and provide safe and convenient pedestrian walkways. [A]
Encourage ridesharing programs. [A]
Improve signal timing to improve circulation. [A]
See MRCOG Access Control Policy for access-controlled arterials and intersections and Development Process Manual for access and intersection spacing standards for non-access controlled arterials. [A]
See Policy 6.1.3 above for auto demand.
See also Policy 6.1.8 above for Commuter Corridors.
See Policy 6.2.3 above for guidance on location of trails and bikeways.
See Land Use Goal 5.1 for Centers & Corridors policies.
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for the Priority Street Elements Matrix.

policies.policyDescription: Freight Network: Prioritize truck routes and rail facilities as identified in the Metropolitan Transportation Plan, providing safe pedestrian crossings at intersections and limiting conflicts with roadways and abutting land uses. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Plan future rail spurs to minimize adverse impact on nearby single-family neighborhoods.
Consider design interventions, such as grade-separated roadways, where there are documented safety issues at railroad crossings.
See Policy 6.6.3 below for freight infrastructure.
See Land Use Policy 5.2.1 for the impacts of development on surrounding communities.
See Urban Design Goal 7.1 for the Priority Street Elements Matrix.
See MTP Map 3-14 for the Primary Freight Network and Truck Restrictions: http://www.mrcog-nm.gov/transportation/metro-planning/long-range-mtp

policies.policyDescription: Aviation: Provide adequate accommodations for domestic travel, shipping, and military purposes. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Sunport Master Plan and Double Eagle II Master Plan.
See MRCOG Joint Land Use Study.

goalDetails.detail: Plan, develop, operate, and maintain a transportation system that provides safe access and mobility for all roadway users.

policies.policyDescription: All Users: Use engineering, education, encouragement, enforcement, and evaluation to improve safety for pedestrians, cyclists, transit users, and motorists. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: For each mode, minimize potential transportation/emergency response hazards such as grade crossings, obsolete street geometry, and inadequate street lighting. [ABC]

Design streets for posted speed limit to align driver behavior with the intended character of the street and to maintain safe vehicular speeds, particularly in areas with high levels of pedestrian activity. [ABC]
Signalize mid-block crossings of major streets at intersections with multi-use trails or bike routes, particularly in locations with high numbers of pedestrians and cyclists. [ABC]
Incorporate on-street and reverse-angle parking to provide traffic calming and enhanced pedestrian and cyclist safety in areas with retail. [A]
See Urban Design Section 7.1.2.4 and Policy 7.4.4 for description of and policy for reverse-angle parking.
Prioritize equity, transparency, and accountability when making investments to improve transportation safety.

policies.policyDescription: Pedestrians: Improve safety for pedestrians through street design. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Improve the comfort and safety of pedestrians in areas with high pedestrian volume, particularly at signalized and unsignalized crosswalks on arterials and collector streets, near schools, and in Centers. [A]
Provide buffers between pedestrians and traffic (e.g. on-street parking, landscaped buffers, etc.). [A]
Prioritize and incentivize public and private pedestrian-scale lighting to increase pedestrian visibility and security. [A]
See Policy 6.2.4 above for safety improvements for pedestrians.
See Policy 6.5.2 below for ADA compliance.
See Urban Design Policy 7.2.1 for policy on pedestrian refuges in medians for wide and high-traffic streets.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.4.2 for ADA compliance.

policies.policyDescription: Cyclists: Improve safety for cyclists through street design. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Policy 6.2.5 Bicycle Network above for safety improvements for cyclists. [A]
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.4.5 for facility planning
See County Pedestrian & Bicycle Safety Action Plan. [BC]
See City Rank 2 Bikeways & Trails Facility Plan. [A]

goalDetails.detail: Promote individual and community health through active transportation, noise mitigation, and air quality protections.

policies.policyDescription: Active Transportation: Promote options and mobility for walking, biking, and other non-motorized travel. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Policy 6.2.3 Pedestrian & Bicycle Connectivity above for pedestrian and bicycle connections and mobility.
See Urban Design Goal 7.2 for policies that promote walkability.

policies.policyDescription: Air Quality: Reduce the adverse effects of automobile travel on air quality through coordinated land use and transportation that promote the efficient placement of housing, employment, and services and improve the viability of multi-modal transportation options. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Coordinate with MRMPO to track performance measures for the regional MTP Preferred Scenario for growth over time. [ABC]
Coordinate with MRMPO and member agencies to work toward cooperative solutions to regional air quality issues. [ABC]
Sustain efforts to convert city fleet vehicles to electric where feasible.
See Policies 6.1.1 and 6.1.3 above for matching transportation and land use and reducing auto demand.
See Goal 6.2 above for policies to encourage a multi-modal system.
See Land Use Goal 5.1 for Centers and Corridors policies.
See also Resilience & Sustainability Goal 13.5 for community health considerations.

policies.policyDescription: Noise: Mitigate traffic noise along roadways using measures that represent a reasonable balance between public expenditure and social, economic, and environmental values of the community. [ABC]

goalDetails.detail: Expand mobility by providing safe and connected networks for non-auto travel and public transit for low-income and vulnerable populations.

policies.policyDescription: Equitable Transportation Systems: Consider the needs of people of all ages and abilities in the design, construction, and operation of transportation systems. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Reduce household transportation costs by improving mobility and choice among modes. [ABC]
Prioritize active transportation investments to underserved and underrepresented neighborhoods to respond to distinct needs within CPAs. [A]
Prioritize transportation investments in Metropolitan Redevelopment Areas and in areas underserved by private development. [A]
See Policy 6.3.1 above for safety improvements.
See Community Identity Goal 4.3 for priorities within each CPA.

policies.policyDescription: ADA: Exceed the minimum requirements of the Americans with Disabilities Act in order to provide reliable and comfortable mobility options. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Provide barrier-free sidewalks and curb ramps.
Design intersections to accommodate people with different mobility levels and/or physical impairments.
Follow national best practices and apply PROWAG where appropriate for transportation infrastructure within the public right-of-way.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.4.2 for ADA compliance.

goalDetails.detail: Invest in a transportation system that stimulates and supports job creation and business development and improves the movement of people, goods, and services.

policies.policyDescription: Accessing Jobs: Align transportation investments to improve connections to Centers and employment clusters. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Policy 6.6.3 below on improving freight connections.
See Land Use Goal 5.1 for development patterns in Centers and along Corridors.
See Land Use Goal 5.4 for policies related to improving the jobs-housing balance.

policies.policyDescription: Transportation Options: Provide a balanced transportation system with effective transportation options to help retain and attract the workforce. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Goal 6.2 above for complete networks and multi-modal priorities.
See Land Use Goal 5.1 for land uses that support a balanced transportation system
See Economic Development Goal 8.1 for attracting talent and businesses.

policies.policyDescription: Freight Movement: Manage congestion, especially along freight corridors, in order to allow efficient movement of goods and services. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Support freight movement so all areas of the city and county have access to goods and services. [ABC]
Improve connectivity of airports to the freight and rail networks and support land uses and development patterns that bolster nearby employment opportunities. [A]
See Policy 6.2.9 above for the freight system.
See Economic Development Goal 8.1 for attracting talent and businesses.

policies.policyDescription: Redevelopment: Leverage transportation investments to spur redevelopment and private investment along commercial corridors and Interstates. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Encourage streetscape and landscape design that provides a positive image at interchanges and access points. [ABC]
Encourage the screening of equipment storage from the public right-of-way. [ABC]
See Goal 6.1 above for land use and transportation integration, including policies about Corridor types.
See Urban Design Policy 7.3.3 for enhancing district identity.
See Urban Design Policy 7.6.2 for matching transportation infrastructure with development context.
See Economic Development Goal 8.1 for attracting talent and businesses.

goalDetails.detail: Implement and maintain an effective and efficient transportation system in a coordinated and cost-effective manner.

policies.policyDescription: Public-Private Coordination: Coordinate public and private sector investment, development, and transportation decisions so that future investments are consistent with the vision and principles of the Comp Plan and the regional MTP. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Integrate all transportation modes as development occurs, coordinating as necessary with property owners, City DMD, County Public Works, ABQ RIDE, MRMPO, and Rio Metro.
Prioritize transportation projects that show high return on investment from lower construction and maintenance costs and higher property values and gross receipts taxes.
Increase the efficiency of existing streets in already developed areas before considering adding new roadway lanes.

policies.policyDescription: Regional Systems: Coordinate across transportation agencies to plan a transportation system for the region. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Coordinate with MRMPO to update and implement the MTP. [ABC]
Follow design recommendations in the LRTS Guide for functional classification and appropriate access management strategies, roadway design guidelines, and guidance on right-of-way width. [ABC]
Coordinate with MRMPO to provide regional connections for on-street bike lanes and multi-use trails. [ABC]

Improve the energy efficiency of the transportation system over time, promoting a variety of transportation modes such as transit, para-transit, and railway systems; bicycle facilities and multi-use trails; and infrastructure for fuel efficient automobiles. [ABC]
Work with MRMPO to assess the adequacy of river crossings based on the population projections and distribution. [ABC]
Coordinate with Rio Metro and MRMPO to provide regional transit service. [A]
See Goal 6.2 above for policies related to mobility and transportation options throughout the region.

policies.policyDescription: Schools: Collaborate with Albuquerque Public Schools and State-chartered schools to provide safe access to school sites for all transportation modes. [ABC]

goalDetails.detail: Provide transportation investments that are responsive to context and natural setting.

policies.policyDescription: Natural and Cultural Context: Locate, design, and construct roads to minimize their impact to natural, historic, archaeological, or other cultural resources, including view corridors. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Design roadways to fit the topography of the area traversed as well as the scale of travel needs. [ABC]
Limit vehicular crossings of arroyos. [A]
Encourage �Scenic Corridors� and/or single-loaded streets as the preferred edges for Major Public Open Space and the Petroglyph National Monument. [A]
See Heritage Conservation Goal 11.3 for cultural landscapes.
See Resilience & Sustainability Policy 13.1.3 for infrastructure design that considers climate change.

policies.policyDescription: Community Planning Areas: Provide transportation investments that are responsive to the distinct needs of each neighborhood and sub-area. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Consider policies and actions for individual corridor priorities within the context of the needs of the broader transportation network.
Consider the needs for priority corridors in each CPA throughout ongoing planning efforts.
See Community Identity Goal 4.3 for CPA priorities.
See Implementation Strategy 2 for CIP prioritization and regional coordination.

goalDetails.detail: Design streetscapes and development form to create a range of environments and experiences for residents and visitors.

policies.policyDescription: Design Elements: Prioritize design elements for transit-supportive design along Premium Transit Corridors, walkability within Centers, and a balance of land use and transportation considerations along other Corridors. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Where Centers and Corridors overlap, follow policies according to the following hierarchy:

subLevels.description: Premium Transit Corridors
Downtown / Urban Centers
Main Streets
Employment / Activity / Village Centers
Major Transit / Multi-Modal / Commuter Corridors

subPolicies.text: Follow policies in Land Use Goals 5.5 and 5.6 for County and City Development Areas for development along corridors not designated in the Comp Plan.
Follow MRMPO�s Long Range Transportation System Guide for street design projects on corridors not designated in the Comp Plan: http://www.mrcog-nm.gov/transportation/metro-planning/long-range-mtp.
See Transportation Goal 6.1 for street projects along designated Corridors.

policies.policyDescription: Development Form: Prioritize elements of development form for each Center and Corridor. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Follow the Development Form Matrix in Table 7-3 for development in Centers.
Follow the Development Form Matrix in Table 7-4 for development within 660 feet of Premium Transit Corridors and elsewhere along Corridors outside of Centers.

policies.policyDescription: Priority Street Elements: Design cross sections according to priorities for street elements within each Corridor, including where the Corridor passes through Centers, especially where right-of-way or budgets may be constrained. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Follow the priority elements for the Travel Way in Table 7-5 based on the Corridor type and location.
Follow the priority elements for the Pedestrian Realm in Table 7-5 based the Corridor type and location.
See Transportation Goal 6.1 for policies about each Corridor.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Goal 12.1 for policies related to infrastructure in the right-of-way.

goalDetails.detail: Increase walkability in all environments, promote pedestrian-oriented development in urban contexts, and increase pedestrian safety in auto-oriented contexts.

policies.policyDescription: Walkability: Ensure convenient and comfortable pedestrian travel. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Improve the pedestrian environment through coordinated design of subdivisions, streets, development sites, and buildings.

Improve pedestrian safety and comfort by providing wider sidewalks, street trees and landscape buffers, lighting, on-street parking, street furniture, and waiting areas and median refuges at large or busy intersections.
Ensure the location and design of sidewalks reflects the existing or planned character and intensity of surrounding land uses.
Enhance existing streets and trails as linear paths connecting destinations throughout the region.
Promote trees and landscape elements in the public right-of-way, along trails, and within private development to ensure a high-quality, pleasant, and healthy built environment.
Discourage gated and/or walled communities and cul-de-sacs.

Design subdivisions to ensure that all residences are no more than � mile from an opening or access point to the major street network so that pedestrians and bicyclists can reach other destinations and/or transit service.
Discourage platting that creates 'wall canyons' along public streets.

Design and place incidental structures such as signs, guywires, poles, fire hydrants, street furniture, and overhead utility wires to minimize visual intrusion and mobility impediment to pedestrians, while still meeting the standards and requirements of the utility or infrastructure.
Emphasize pedestrian connections between buildings on a site and to adjacent uses.
See Land Use Policy 5.2.1 for land use patterns that support walkability.
See Transportation Policies 6.2.3 and 6.2.4 for pedestrian connections.
See Transportation Policy 6.3.1 and Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.4.2 for ADA considerations.

policies.policyDescription: Walkable Places: Promote high-quality pedestrian-oriented neighborhoods and districts as the essential building blocks of a sustainable region. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Design streetscapes to incorporate street trees, landscape elements, and enhanced sidewalks to support vibrant pedestrian environments. [ABC]
Encourage building and site design that activates the pedestrian environment through building frontage, entrances, parking areas, and gathering spaces. [A]
Support pedestrian activity along streets, including sidewalk dining, parquitos/parklets, and open streets events. [A]
See Policy 7.1.3 above for priorities in the pedestrian realm in Centers and along Corridors to improve walkability.

goalDetails.detail: Reinforce sense of place through context-sensitive design of development and streetscapes.

policies.policyDescription: Natural and Cultural Features: Preserve, enhance, and leverage natural features and views of cultural landscapes. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Minimize alteration of existing vegetation and topography in subdivision and site design.
Provide appropriate transitions to Open Space.
See Policy 7.5.1 below for landscape design.
See Land Use Policy 5.3.4 for conservation development.
See Parks & Open Space Goal 10.3 for policies to protect natural features.
See Heritage Conservation Goal 11.3 for policies about cultural landscapes and development adjacent to natural features.
See Resilience & Sustainability Goal 13.4 for policies to protect natural resources.

policies.policyDescription: Community Character: Encourage design strategies that recognize and embrace the character differences that give communities their distinct identities and make them safe and attractive places. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Design development to reflect the character of the surrounding area and protect and enhance views. [ABC]
Encourage development and site design that incorporates CPTED principles. [ABC]
Reinforce identity through distinctive streetscape elements such as interpretive and gateway signage, wayfinding elements, lighting, and banners. [ABC]
Develop streetscape standards for rural development contexts that preserve historic character, including gravel roads, drainage swales, and no curbs. [ABC]
Encourage high-quality development that capitalizes on predominant architectural styles, building materials, and landscape elements. [A]
See Goal 7.2 above for design approaches to create walkable communities.
See Goal 7.6 below for context-sensitive infrastructure.
See Community Identity Goal 4.3 for character-defining elements of each CPA.
See Land Use Goal 5.2 for policies about Complete Communities.
See Heritage Conservation Goal 11.3 for cultural landscapes and view protection.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.3.4 for public safety.

policies.policyDescription: Placemaking: Encourage efforts to establish and strengthen district identity within Centers, business districts, and neighborhoods. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Identify, celebrate, and leverage special places.
Distinguish district gateways and entrances with signage, lighting, monuments, etc.
Develop distinctive signage, banners, and logos to use on businesses, in parks, near gateways and entrances, and on plazas.
Prioritize capital projects to address key issues and leverage key assets.
Encourage site design that includes gathering spaces for festivals, markets, and events.
See Community Identity Goal 4.1 for policies to preserve and enhance neighborhood identity.
See Economic Development Goal 8.1 for additional policies on placemaking.
See Heritage Conservation Goal 11.5 for policies on cultural events, public art, and programming civic spaces.

policies.policyDescription: Infill: Promote infill that enhances the built environment or blends in style and building materials with surrounding structures and the streetscape of the block in which it is located. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: For Activity and Village Centers, ensure that infill and redevelopment is compatible with the character of the surrounding context and similar in height, mass, and volume to adjacent development. [ABC]

Promote buildings and massing of commercial and office uses adjacent to single-family neighborhoods that is neighborhood-scale, well-designed, appropriately located, and consistent with the existing development context and neighborhood character. [A]
See Community Identity Goal 4.1 for policies to preserve and enhance neighborhood identity.
See Land Use Goals 5.5 and 5.6 for Development Area policies.
See Land Use Policies 5.1.2 and 5.3.1 for policies related to infill development.

policies.policyDescription: Development Quality: Encourage innovative and high quality design in all development. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Community Identity Goal 4.1 for character protections in neighborhoods.
See Economic Development Policy 8.2.3 for supporting innovative and sustainable business.
See Housing Policy 9.2.2 for innovative and high quality housing.

goalDetails.detail: Design parking facilities to match the development context and complement the surrounding built environment.

policies.policyDescription: Parking Strategies: Provide parking options, optimize parking efficiencies, and plan for parking as essential infrastructure. [A]

subPolicies.text: Support �park once and walk� opportunities.
Encourage shared parking.
Minimize overflow parking in residential areas, particularly in areas near Centers, institutions, and Open Space.

policies.policyDescription: Parking Requirements: Establish off-street parking requirements based on development context. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Discourage oversized parking facilities.
In urban development contexts and within walking distance of high-capacity or high-frequency transit stops or stations, lower parking requirements as follows:

subLevels.description: Lower parking requirements for development within 330� of a shared parking structure.
Lower parking requirements for development adjacent to high-frequency transit or within 1/4-mile of a high-capacity transit station or transit center.
Credit on-street parking toward parking requirements, except where residential parking permits are used.
Allow parking requirements to be met through �in-lieu� fees earmarked for a future shared parking facility, such as a shared lot, structure, or reverse-angle parking improvements.

subPolicies.text: In Suburban and Rural development contexts, provide adequate parking to serve land uses on a daily basis and ensure design that accommodates pedestrians and activates edges.

policies.policyDescription: Off-street Parking Design: Encourage well-designed, efficient, safe, and attractive parking facilities. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Locate off-street parking to respond appropriately for each Center and Corridor per Tables 7-3 and 7-4. [ABC]
Incorporate trees, vegetation, and pervious surfaces in parking areas to mitigate environmental impacts, minimize heat and glare, and improve aesthetics. [ABC]
Ensure safe pedestrian pathways in parking areas that connect to building entrances, adjacent roadways, and adjacent sites. [ABC]
Break up large parking lots into smaller parking fields with planting areas. [ABC]
Discourage parking abutting the travel way to allow more active uses near the public right-of-way. [ABC]
Provide visual interest or screening on parking structure fa�ades and additional visual enhancements such as landscape elements and/or public art at ground level. [A]
Encourage street-front parking structures to provide additional activity at street level, such as liner buildings or public spaces. [A]

policies.policyDescription: On-street Parking: Support on-street parking in urban and pedestrian-oriented development contexts to serve adjacent uses, reduce the need for off-street parking, slow auto traffic speeds, and buffer the pedestrian realm. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Ensure that on-street parking preserves the clear sight triangle, traffic flow, and safe pedestrian crossings. [ABC]
Encourage reverse-angle parking as the preferred parking arrangement where permitted by available right-of-way, as it provides the best sightlines for drivers to see cyclists and other vehicles. [A]

goalDetails.detail: Design sites, buildings, and landscape elements to respond to the high desert environment.

policies.policyDescription: Landscape Design: Encourage landscape treatments that are consistent with the high desert climate to enhance our sense of place. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Design landscape and site improvements to complement the individual site, the overall appearance of the corridor, and surrounding land uses.
Design landscapes and vegetation to be consistent with the microclimate of the site location as well as within the site.
Discourage planting of higher water use species outside of riparian microclimates, the Mid Rio Grande Conservancy District, or areas served by swales.
Incorporate xeric site design principles to establish an oasis area and transition areas, identify beneficial placement for plant species, and maximize shade in summer months.
See Policy 7.3.1 above for protection of natural features that contribute to sense of place.
See Community Identity Policy 4.1.3 for placemaking efforts to preserve and enhance neighborhoods.
See Heritage Conservation Goal 11.3 for cultural landscapes.
See Resilience & Sustainability Goal 13.2 for policies about water supply and quality.
See Resilience & Sustainability Policy 13.5.2 for healthful development.

policies.policyDescription: Site Design: Incorporate local climate conditions into site design. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Support the use of sustainable building materials. [ABC]

Design outdoor spaces to use landscape elements, shade, openings for winter sun, and non-glare materials advantageously to create inviting and comfortable places for people to gather in all seasons. [ABC]
Encourage climate-sensitive design of buildings, entrances, plazas, walkways, drainage, and sites to conserve and harvest rainwater, provide shade, and protect pedestrians from rain and wind. [A]
See Resilience & Sustainability Policy 13.5.2 for healthful development.

goalDetails.detail: Match infrastructure design to intended densities and development patterns to minimize lifecycle costs and conserve natural resources.

policies.policyDescription: Stormwater Treatments: Match stormwater treatment techniques and practices to the density/intensity of land use and development context. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Support shared drainage management facilities and Low Impact Development (LID) techniques in urban development contexts.

Prioritize natural stormwater treatments and management facilities, such as bioswales, linear ponds, etc., in rural and suburban development contexts, particularly in the public right-of-way and parking areas to provide flood control and to improve stormwater quality.
See Heritage Conservation Policies 11.1.3 and 11.3.2 for acequias and arroyos.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policies 12.1.4 and 12.1.5 for drainage and ditches.
See Resilience & Sustainability Goal 13.2 for policies about water quality and supply.

policies.policyDescription: Transportation Infrastructure: Match infrastructure capacity, design, and maintenance to the development context, expected land use intensities of abutting development, and all travel modes. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Design sites to coordinate auto access, circulation, and building placement to minimize harmful effects of traffic on single-family neighborhoods adjacent to major streets.
Employ street trees, barriers, buffering, and other landscape design methods to minimize the effect of traffic on adjacent uses.
See Policy 7.3.1 above for streetscape design that contributes to sense of place.
See Land Use Goal 5.1 for expected land uses in Centers and along Corridors.
See Transportation Goal 6.2 for multi-modal systems.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.1.4 related to stormwater runoff.

policies.policyDescription: Utility Infrastructure: Encourage design of visible infrastructure (surface and overhead) that respects the character of neighborhoods and communities and protects significant natural and cultural features. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Work with ABCWUA to design facilities that blend into the natural landscape and include native or naturalized vegetation.
Minimize disturbance to environmentally sensitive areas, such as Major Public Open Space and cultural landscapes, and minimize visual impact of utilities with careful siting and design.
Select street tree species that are suitable in size to minimize interference with electric utilities in locations with overhead lines.
See Community Identity Goal 4.3 for character-defining elements of each CPA.
See Heritage Conservation Goal 11.3 for cultural landscapes and view protection.
See Parks & Open Space Goal 10.3 for Open Space policies.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Goal 12.1 for policies about utility infrastructure.

goalDetails.detail: Create places where business and talent will stay and thrive.

policies.policyDescription: Diverse Places: Foster a range of interesting places and contexts with different development intensities, densities, uses, and building scale to encourage economic development opportunities. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Invest in Centers and Corridors to concentrate a variety of employment opportunities for a range of occupational skills and salary levels. [ABC]
Support a variety of lower-density, lower-intensity services, jobs, and housing outside of Centers. [ABC]
Protect natural resources, including land and resources necessary for agricultural economic development in rural areas. [BC]
Invest in Downtown to increase its viability as a regional employment center. [A]
See Community Identity Goals 4.1 and 4.3 for protections for existing neighborhoods.
See Land Use Goals 5.1 and 5.2 for policies about where and how growth and development should occur.
See Land Use Goal 5.7 for regulatory approaches to promote development that furthers the goals and policies of the Comp Plan.
See Urban Design Goal 7.3 for policies that reinforce sense of place.
See Parks & Open Space chapter for outdoor and recreation opportunities as part of our diverse places.
See Heritage Conservation chapter for cultural assets and traditions.

policies.policyDescription: Resilient Economy: Encourage economic development efforts that improve quality of life for new and existing residents and foster a robust, resilient, and diverse economy. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Maximize opportunities for economic development that furthers social, cultural, and environmental goals.
Encourage the production, local sale, and export of locally-grown and made goods.
Prioritize local job creation, employer recruitment, and support for development projects that hire local residents.
Grow the community�s economic base through recruitment, retention/expansion, and new business startups to bring additional income into the region.
Encourage livable wages and high-quality work environments.
Coordinate with schools, universities, vocational programs, and workforce training providers to build worker capacity and skills.

policies.policyDescription: Economic Base: Strengthen and diversify the economic base to help reduce reliance on government spending. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Promote and support local export-based businesses (e.g. manufacturing) as a way to stimulate local economic activity.
Prioritize infrastructure improvements to support business development in areas with high potential for employment opportunities, such as the I-25 Corridor and West Side Employment Centers.
Recruit new export-based businesses to expand and diversify the economic base.
Focus economic development strategies, programs, and activities to support existing and emerging economic base industry clusters that are important to the region.
See Transportation Goal 6.2 for multi-modal priorities.
See Transportation Goal 6.6 for transportation investments, including freight, to support economic development.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Goal 12.1 for infrastructure systems, including information technology.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Goal 12.5 for policies to guide public resource allocation.

policies.policyDescription: Leverage Assets: Enhance and market the region�s unique characteristics internally and to outside businesses and individuals in order to compete with other regions. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Encourage development that leverages the history and character of special places, such as Route 66 and Old Town.
Promote the Open Space network and cultural landscapes as assets and quality of life amenities for tourism and recruitment efforts for businesses and talent.
See Community Identity Goal 4.3 for assets identified during the Community Planning Area (CPA) assessments.
See Land Use Policy 5.2.1 for desired land uses
See Land Use Policies 5.7.2 and 5.7.4 for regulations and processes that promote development that supports the Comp Plan vision.
See Parks & Open Space chapter for parks, Open Space, and recreational assets.
See Heritage Conservation chapter for cultural assets.

policies.policyDescription: Available Land: Maintain sufficient land that is appropriately zoned to accommodate projected employment growth in targeted areas. [ABC]

goalDetails.detail: Foster a culture of creativity and entrepreneurship and encourage private businesses to grow.

policies.policyDescription: Local Business: Emphasize local business development. [ABC]
Diverse Talent: Promote a more inclusive ecosystem for developing entrepreneurs. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Encourage entrepreneurship among traditionally underrepresented segments of the community, including multilingual people, women, and veterans.
Promote efforts to reach potential entrepreneurs in the neighborhoods and industry sectors where they work.
Support neighborhood-based capacity building for potential entrepreneurs.
See Policy 8.1.1 above for development that encourages economic development opportunities.
See Land Use Goals 5.1 and 5.2 for policies about where and how growth and development should occur.

policies.policyDescription: Sustainable Business: Provide incentives for development projects and businesses that have sustainable economic characteristics. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Encourage innovative, energy efficient design and construction, standards, and techniques.
Promote local hiring, higher-wages, and business that contribute to the economic base (export-based).
Cluster compatible businesses to allow for more efficient movement of goods, services, and workers.
Promote businesses that have economic qualities and/or products that support sustainability.

policies.policyDescription: Public Funds: Leverage public funds and efforts to support venture capital and private investment. [ABC]
Creative Economy: Promote the creative economy. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Heritage Conservation Policy 11.1.1 for economic activity related to agriculture.
See Heritage Conservation Goal 11.5 for policies about public art and cultural programs.

policies.policyDescription: Job Training: Support existing entrepreneurship, education, training, and programs. [ABC]

goalDetails.detail: Ensure a sufficient supply and range of high-quality housing types that meet current and future needs at a variety of price levels to ensure more balanced housing options.

policies.policyDescription: Housing Options: Support the development, improvement, and conservation of housing for a variety of income levels and types of residents and households. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Increase the supply of housing that is affordable for all income levels. [ABC]
Assure against discrimination in the provision of housing. [ABC]
Assure the availability of a wide distribution of quality housing for all persons regardless of race, color, religion, sex, national origin, ancestry, age, or disabled status. [ABC]
Protect the quality of existing housing stock through rehabilitation programs and training. [ABC]
Provide for the development of quality housing for elderly residents. [ABC]
Encourage community compounds to support multi-generational housing where such traditional development patterns exist. [ABC]
Ameliorate the problems of homelessness, overcrowding, and displacement of low income residents. [ABC]
Maintain an affordable housing supply in neighborhoods, in addition to creating market-rate housing, as part of revitalization efforts. [A]
Provide for the development of multi-family housing close to public services, transit, and shopping. [A]
Work on conservation, improvement, and expansion of the housing available to low- and moderate-income families until all housing in the area meets City Housing Code standards. [A]
Promote access to programs that give incentives for energy efficiency improvements to low-income households.
See Goals 9.4 and 9.5 below for policies that address homelessness and vulnerable populations.
See Land Use Policies 5.1.1 and 5.2.1 on appropriate locations for different housing densities.
See Land Use Goal 5.3 for efficient development patterns.

policies.policyDescription: Affordability: Provide for mixed-income neighborhoods by encouraging high-quality, affordable and mixed income housing options throughout the area. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Prioritize support for affordable housing that the market is unable to provide for populations with the lowest income levels and/or special needs.
Encourage a diversity of housing types, such as live/work spaces, stacked flats, townhouses, urban apartments, lofts, accessory dwelling units, and condominiums.
Encourage housing types that maintain the scale of existing single-family neighborhoods while expanding housing options.
Encourage the development of higher-density affordable and mixed-income housing in Downtown, near job centers, and along transit corridors.
Encourage mixed-use development that includes non-residential uses and the opportunity for access to services.
See Community Identity Goal 4.1 for policies to maintain neighborhood character.
See Land Use Policies 5.1.1 and 5.2.1 on appropriate locations for different housing densities.
See Land Use Goal 5.3 for efficient development patterns.

policies.policyDescription: Fair Housing: Promote fair housing through local housing programs that enhance housing affordability, choice and access to opportunity for all communities, especially those communities that bear the burdens from lack of investment and access to opportunity. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Enhance housing choice for people in federally defined protected classes throughout the region by coordinating plans and investments to affirmatively further fair housing. [ABC]
Promote equitable housing practices for federally defined protected classes through fair housing education and enforcement. [ABC]
Evaluate the effect of development trends, policies, and regulations on housing costs as part of the CPA assessment process and recommend adjustments to avoid displacement of existing residents. [A]
See Goals 9.4 and 9.5 below for policies that address homelessness and vulnerable populations.
See Heritage Conservation Policy 11.2.1 about displacement and gentrification.

goalDetails.detail: Promote housing design that is sustainable and compatible with the natural and built environments.

policies.policyDescription: Compatibility: Encourage housing development that enhances neighborhood character, maintains compatibility with surrounding land uses, and responds to its development context � i.e. urban, suburban, or rural � with appropriate densities, site design, and relationship to the street. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Community Identity Goals 4.1 and 4.2 for policies on neighborhood character.
See Land Use Policy 5.2.1 for land use compatibility.
See Transportation Goal 6.8 for policies related to street design in different contexts.
See Urban Design Goal 7.3 for policies on designing the interaction with the public realm.

policies.policyDescription: High Quality: Encourage quality and innovation in new housing design and construction, materials, and energy and water conservation. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Land Use Policy 5.3.8 for solar protections.
See Urban Design Goal 7.5 for context-sensitive site design.

policies.policyDescription: Cluster Housing: Encourage housing developments that cluster residential units in order to provide community gathering spaces and/or open space. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Encourage innovative and diverse options for intentional or communal living. [ABC]
See Land Use Policies 5.3.3 and 5.3.4 on cluster and conservation development.

goalDetails.detail: Support increased housing density in appropriate places with adequate services and amenities.

policies.policyDescription: Centers & Corridors: Encourage higher density, multi-unit housing and mixed-use development in Downtown, Urban, Activity, and Village Centers, and along Premium and Major Transit Corridors to capture growth, relieve development pressure at the edge of the urban footprint, and maintain low densities in rural areas. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Land Use Goal 5.1 for land use policies related to Centers and Corridors.
See Transportation Goal 6.1 for policies related to Corridors.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.5.3 for prioritization of funding to support desired growth patterns.

policies.policyDescription: Other Areas: Increase housing density and housing options in other areas by locating near appropriate uses and services and maintaining the scale of surrounding development. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Encourage higher-density residential and mixed-use development as appropriate uses near existing public facilities, educational facilities, job centers, social services, and shopping districts.
Encourage multi-family and mixed-use development in areas where a transition is needed between single-family homes and more intense development.

Allow accessory dwelling units in areas with existing infrastructure capacity, where intergenerational living is encouraged, where walkability is encouraged, and where affordable housing is needed in single-family neighborhoods.
See Land Use Goals 5.5 and 5.6 for policies related to Development Areas in the city and county.

goalDetails.detail: Make homelessness rare, short-term, and non-recurring.

policies.policyDescription: Best Practices: Implement an appropriate and effective model to address chronic homelessness. [ABC]
Services: Provide expanded options for shelters and services for people experiencing temporary homelessness. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Land Use Policy 5.3.7 for objectionable land uses.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Goal 12.3 for service provision.

policies.policyDescription: Equitable Distribution: Support a network of service points that are easily accessible by residents and workers, geographically distributed throughout the city and county, and proximate to transit. [ABC]

goalDetails.detail: Expand capacity to provide quality housing and services to vulnerable populations.

policies.policyDescription: Quality Housing: Ensure well-maintained, safe transitional and permanent housing for the lowest-income households that are most at risk of homelessness. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Work with funding agencies to identify and leverage potential sources to provide housing for extremely low-income renters.
Support demonstration projects that use innovative approaches to leverage alternative funding sources.
Prioritize lowest-income households when allocating local funds for affordable and workforce housing.
Encourage housing developers to coordinate with supportive service providers.

policies.policyDescription: Transitional Services: Encourage on-site transitional services with culturally competent service delivery that respects the dignity of individuals and families and fosters self-determination and self-sufficiency, including job training, financial education, and behavioral health assistance. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Land Use Policy 5.3.7 for objectionable land uses.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Goal 12.3 for service provision.

goalDetails.detail: Promote cost-effective housing redevelopment and construction that meets community needs.

policies.policyDescription: Development Cost: Reduce development costs and balance short-term benefits of delivering less costly housing with long-term benefits of preserving investment in homes and protecting quality of life. [ABC]

Incentives: Provide incentives for developing affordable housing for low- and moderate-income households, by aligning development regulations, infrastructure requirements, and fee structures with the priorities of City and County affordable housing programs. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Goal 9.1 above for desired housing types and affordability.
See Land Use Goal 5.7 for strategies to align development with the goals of the Comp Plan.

goalDetails.detail: Coordinate strategic deployment of housing-related funds and partnerships with community-based organizations for projects that achieve housing goals.

policies.policyDescription: Housing Coordination: Coordinate with affordable housing non-profits, developers, advocates, service providers, and other stakeholders to leverage available funds and planning efforts to address affordable housing, homelessness, and services for vulnerable populations. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Promote home ownership in all neighborhoods through collaboration with lenders and nonprofit organizations, including home ownership for low to moderate income buyers and first time buyers.
Encourage renovation and rehabilitation to preserve and enhance the existing housing stock in older neighborhoods.

policies.policyDescription: Metropolitan Redevelopment: Identify and prioritize opportunities for catalytic projects that stabilize and serve blighted neighborhoods and support redevelopment in those areas. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Leverage partnerships with the private sector and community-based organizations as needed.
Encourage development of mixed-use and mixed-income projects.
Pursue remediation and redevelopment of brownfield sites when feasible.

goalDetails.detail: Provide parks, Open Space, and recreation facilities that meet the needs of all residents and use natural resources responsibly.

policies.policyDescription: Distribution: Improve the community�s access to recreational opportunities by balancing the City and County�s parks and Open Space systems with the built environment. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Protect and maintain a high-quality, accessible system of recreation facilities and sites sufficient to serve all areas.
Establish an interconnected network of parks, Open Space, and trails with safe pedestrian connections to community facilities, neighborhoods, and Centers.
Locate recreational facilities within Centers to address gaps in service and to be accessible by all modes of transportation, particularly foot and bicycle.
Encourage public open spaces and facilities in new developments to locate near active uses � commercial and community services, employment, and schools.
Coordinate park locations with drainage ways and local street alignments to maximize access to nearby residential neighborhoods without needing to cross an arterial road.
See Policies 10.2.3 and 10.4.4 below related to trail connections.
See Transportation Goal 6.2 for policies related to multi-use trails.

policies.policyDescription: Universal Design: Plan, design, program, and maintain parks, Open Space, and recreation facilities for use by people of all age groups and physical abilities. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Design and maintain landscaping and park features appropriate to the location, function, public expectations, and intensity of use. [ABC]
Encourage subdivision platting that maximizes park and Open Space access and visibility. [A]
See Transportation Policy 6.5.2 for accessibility and mobility within the public right-of-way.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.4.2 for additional ADA policies.

policies.policyDescription: Agriculture: Support food production and other agricultural uses in parks, Open Space, and public rights-of-way. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Land Use Policies 5.5.3 and 5.5.4 for agricultural protection in County Rural and Semi-Urban Development Areas.
See Heritage Conservation Policy 11.1.1 for agricultural protection.

policies.policyDescription: Water Conservation: Employ low-water use and reclamation strategies to conserve water. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Incorporate native vegetation and low water use species wherever possible, particularly in areas without easy access to irrigation. [ABC]
Integrate irrigation, water conservation, drainage, and flood control functions within parks and Open Spaces with ecological preservation and recreational purposes. [ABC]
Incorporate natural drainage and infiltration techniques to irrigate parks, golf courses, and Open Space. [A]

goalDetails.detail: Provide opportunities for outdoor education, recreation, and cultural activities that meet community needs, enhance quality of life, and promote community involvement for all residents.

policies.policyDescription: Park Types: Plan and implement a system of parks to meet a range of needs at different scales, including small neighborhood parks, community parks, active parks, regional parks, and linear parks. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Coordinate joint use and capital improvement programs to maximize effective use of tax payer dollars.
Acquire, develop, and maintain major regional parks suitable for larger sports complexes or event spaces with convenient access to serve the wider region.
Encourage developers to design, develop, and maintain parks through density bonuses and other incentives.
Prioritize parks planning and implementation as early as possible in the development process in fast-growing or underserved areas.
See Community Identity Goal 4.3 for priorities in each Community Planning Area.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.4.4 for co-location of facilities.

policies.policyDescription: Security: Increase safety and security in parks. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Minimize vandalism through adequate lighting, site design, and durable materials. [ABC]
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.3.4 on public safety.

policies.policyDescription: Multi-use Trails: Connect parks by designing, building, and maintaining trails to accepted standards. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Policy 10.4.4 below for trails along arroyos, ditches, and acequias.
See Transportation Goal 6.2 for policies related to multi-use trails.

goalDetails.detail: Protect the integrity and quality of the region�s natural features and environmental assets and provide opportunities for outdoor recreation and education.

policies.policyDescription: Open Space Acquisition: Acquire significant lands throughout the community to shape the urban form, conserve natural and cultural resources, and protect agricultural land. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Collaborate on Open Space acquisition to implement the Open Space network.
Acquire trail, wildlife, and drainage corridors as important elements of the Open Space network.
Acquire lands prone to natural hazards as well as adequate buffers for public safety.
Acquire lands abutting significant features to ensure adequate protections of natural and cultural resources.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.4.5 for Rank 2 Facility Plans that guide Open Space acquisition and maintenance.
See Resilience & Sustainability Policy 13.4.4 for protecting areas with unique landforms and habitats.

policies.policyDescription: Preservation: Identify and manage sensitive lands within the Open Space network to protect their ecological function. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Manage public access to best protect natural resources.
Ensure that development within Open Space is compatible with its preservation purpose.
See Heritage Conservation Goal 11.3 for cultural landscapes and development adjacent to open space.

policies.policyDescription: Use: Provide low-impact recreational and educational opportunities consistent with the carrying capacity of the Open Space resource. [ABC]

Bosque and Rio Grande: Carefully design access to the Rio Grande, the Bosque, and surrounding river lands to provide entry to those portions suitable for recreational, scientific, and educational purposes, while controlling access in other more sensitive areas to preserve the natural wildlife habitat and maintain essential watershed management and drainage functions. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Minimize disturbance or removal of existing natural vegetation from the Bosque.
See Policy 10.1.4 above related to balancing water management functions with ecological preservation and recreation.
See Heritage Conservation Policy 11.3.3 for policies related to land development adjacent to the Bosque.

policies.policyDescription: Petroglyph National Monument: Preserve the volcanoes, key portions of the basalt flow, and the Northwest Mesa Escarpment as part of the Open Space network. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Identify and conserve the unique environmental, visual, recreational, archaeological, and historical qualities and opportunities of the Northwest Mesa Escarpment. [A]
Conserve the Escarpment as an entire unit with a recognizable relationship to the volcanoes that created it, the mesa top which borders it, and the arroyos that bisect it. [A]
Design public access and public facilities to be compatible with the sensitive nature of the Escarpment. [A]
Acquire land suitable for MPOS as funding becomes available to help conserve important ecological and archaeological features and include hiking trails. [A]

Limit utilities and roads to areas that are least sensitive to disturbance, avoiding the following areas: Piedras Marcadas Canyon, the point where the mid branch of the San Antonio crosses the Escarpment, the Marsh peninsula, Rinconada Canyon, and the escarpment immediately south of Rinconada Canyon. [A]
See Heritage Conservation Policies 11.3.4 and 11.3.6 related to development on lands within and adjacent to the Monument and on the Northwest Mesa.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policies 12.1.4 and 12.1.5 for policies related to drainage infrastructure improvements that cross the Monument.

policies.policyDescription: Escarpments: Preserve the ceja and Mesa del Sol escarpments as part of the Open Space network. [A]

goalDetails.detail: Coordinate across disciplines, jurisdictions, and geographies to leverage limited resources, maximize efficiencies, and best serve the public�s need for parks and recreation facilities.

policies.policyDescription: Public Priorities: Engage the public to determine priorities and ensure equitable public investment. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Coordinate with the City Open Space Division, Open Space Advisory Board, and other interested parties to clarify the intent for the Open Space trust lands adjacent to Double Eagle II Airport. [A]
See Community Identity Goal 4.2 for policies about community engagement, CPA assessments, and City Leaders programs.

policies.policyDescription: System Planning: Coordinate among departments and across jurisdictional boundaries to plan interconnected networks, manage natural resources, leverage public investment, eliminate gaps in service, and avoid duplication of effort. [ABC]

Co-located Facilities: Maximize opportunities for multi-functional, co-located, and joint use of compatible parks, Open Space, trails, and recreation facilities to best leverage public investment. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Policy 10.4.4 below for trails along arroyos, ditches, and acequias.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.4.4 for co-location of facilities.

policies.policyDescription: Arroyos and Drainage: Work with MRGCD and AMAFCA to protect arroyos, drains, and acequias as part of Community Green Space. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Acquire, regulate, and manage trail corridors to protect natural features, views, drainage, and other functions and/or to link other areas within the Open Space network. [ABC]
Protect drainage or Open Space functions of arroyos from development impacts. [ABC]
Use natural treatments for unchannelized arroyos. [ABC]
Design and maintain irrigation ditches and acequias to be compatible with neighborhood character. [ABC]
Encourage trails along suitable arroyos and irrigation ditches and design the facilities to protect the irrigation and drainage function. [ABC]
Employ safety measures, such as buffers and railings, for trail users. [ABC]
Establish maintenance responsibilities for arroyos within and outside of Open Space. [ABC]
Preserve arroyos to encourage residents� connections with nature. [A]
Connect the Open Space and trails network to suitable arroyos, drains, and acequias. [A]
See Policies 10.1.1 and 10.2.3 above about trails linking the parks network.
See Community Identity Goals 4.2 and 4.3 for the CPA assessment process and character-defining elements in each CPA.
See Land Use Policies 5.3.3 and 5.3.4 for policies that encourage cluster development to avoid the floodplain.
See Land Use Policies 5.5.1 and 5.6.1 for Community Green Space.
See Transportation Goal 6.2 for policies related to multi-use trails.
See Urban Design Goal 7.6.1 for stormwater infrastructure design considerations.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policies 12.1.4 and 12.1.5 for policies related to drainage infrastructure.
See Resilience & Sustainability Policy 13.4.4 for protecting areas with unique landforms and habitats.

goalDetails.detail: Preserve and enhance farmland, the acequia system, and traditional communities.

policies.policyDescription: Agricultural Preservation: Promote and assist the viability of agricultural heritage, including the conservation and use of irrigated agricultural land, small-scale agriculture, and open space in the valley and other areas. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Conserve natural resources that are vital to agricultural economic activities.
Promote the acequia system as a vital component in the life of the community and support its living tradition and active use.
Recognize and support inheritance traditions and coordinate with traditional community organizations.
See Policy 11.1.3 below for acequia protection.
See Policy 11.1.4 below for local heritage protection.
See Community Identity Goals 4.2 and 4.3 for policies that promote community participation and protection of character-defining elements.
See Land Use Policy 5.2.1 for desired land uses.
See Land Use Policies 5.3.3 and 5.3.4 for conservation development.
See Economic Development Policies 8.1.1 and 8.2.1 for promotion of agriculture and local business.
See Parks & Open Space Policy 10.1.3 for agriculture on public lands
See Parks & Open Space Policy 10.4.4 for preservation of arroyos and drainage systems.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policies 12.1.4 and 12.1.5 for drainage infrastructure.
See Resilience & Sustainability Goal 13.2 for policies on water supply and quality.

policies.policyDescription: Rural Character: Protect the character of rural areas and ensure that development is sensitive to historic and cultural patterns. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Recognize the Valley as a unique and fragile resource and as an inestimable and irreplaceable part of the entire metropolitan community.
Protect and preserve historic properties, special places, and important aspects of rural character such as irrigated fields, acequias, narrow roadways, and tree-lined streets.
See Community Identity Goals 4.2 and 4.3 for policies that promote community participation and protection of character-defining elements.
See Land Use Policy 5.5.3 for Rural Areas in the county.
See Transportation Goal 6.8 for context-sensitive road design.
See Urban Design Goal 7.3 for policies that protect an area�s sense of place through design.
See Resilience & Sustainability Policy 13.4.4 for protecting unique landforms and habitats.

policies.policyDescription: Acequia Preservation: Support efforts to protect and preserve the acequia system for agricultural and low-impact recreation purposes and strengthen connections with adjacent neighborhoods and development. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Land Use Policies 5.5.1 and 5.6.1 for development adjacent to acequias.
See Urban Design Policy 7.6.1 for design treatments on stormwater infrastructure.
See Parks & Open Space Policy 10.4.4 for preservation of arroyos and drainage systems.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities and Services Policies 12.1.4 and 12.1.5 for drainage infrastructure.

policies.policyDescription: Local Heritage: Keep local traditions and heritage alive and cultivate neighborhoods and rural areas as safe and excellent places to live and raise families. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Encourage efforts and programs that respond to local needs and revitalize neighborhood traditions.
Encourage collaboration among jurisdictions, businesses, and residents along El Camino Real to acknowledge and preserve the trail�s importance to local heritage.
See Community Identity Goal 4.3 for character-defining elements of each Community Planning Area.
See Land Use Policy 5.5.3 for Rural Areas in the county.
See Transportation Goal 6.8 for context-sensitive road design.
See Urban Design Goal 7.3 for policies that protect an area�s sense of place through design.

goalDetails.detail: Preserve and enhance significant historic districts and buildings to reflect our past as we move into the future and to strengthen our sense of identity.

policies.policyDescription: Gentrification: Balance the objectives of historic preservation and conservation of affordable housing. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Work to maintain a range of housing options and affordability levels to ameliorate the displacement of low income households.
Encourage renovation and rehabilitation to preserve and enhance the existing housing stock.

policies.policyDescription: Historic Registration: Promote the preservation of historic buildings and districts determined to be of significant local, State, and/or National historical interest. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Preserve and maintain historically significant buildings and spaces.
Recognize historic buildings and districts as vital elements of the community.
Encourage the adaptive reuse of historic structures as a strategy to preserve character and encourage reinvestment.
See Community Identity Goal 4.3 for character-defining elements of each Community Planning Area.

policies.policyDescription: Distinct Built Environments: Preserve and enhance the social, cultural, and historical features that contribute to the identities of distinct communities, neighborhoods, and districts. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Consider local history and the visual environment, particularly features unique to Albuquerque, as significant determinants in development and redevelopment decisions in light of their relationship to and effect upon the following:

subLevels.description: Architectural styles and traditions;
Current and historic significance to Albuquerque;
Historic plazas and Centers;
Culture, traditions, celebrations, and events

subPolicies.text: Encourage development that strengthens the identity and cohesiveness of the surrounding community and enhances distinct historic and cultural features.
Design streets and streetscapes that match the distinctive character of historic areas.
See Community Identity Goals 4.1 and 4.3 for character protections and character-defining elements of each Community Planning Area.
See Urban Design Goal 7.3 for policies related to sense of place.

goalDetails.detail: Protect, reuse, and/or enhance significant cultural landscapes as important contributors to our heritage and rich and complex identities.

policies.policyDescription: Natural and Cultural Features: Preserve and enhance the natural and cultural characteristics and features that contribute to the distinct identity of communities, neighborhoods, and cultural landscapes. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Minimize negative impacts and maximize enhancements and design that complement the natural environment, particularly features unique to Albuquerque, in development and redevelopment in light of the relationship to and effect upon the following:

subLevels.description: Indigenous vegetation and other materials appropriate to landscapes;
Topography and landscape features such as arroyos, the Rio Grande and Bosque, the foothills, and escarpments;
Soils and erosion potential;
Colors and textures of the natural environment; and
Scenic views from the public right-of-way

subPolicies.text: Minimize the visibility of structures in highly scenic areas and on the western horizon as seen throughout the city through building design and materials that blend with the natural colors of the landscape and limit reflectivity.
Protect important views from public rights-of-way through regulations on street orientation, site layout, building height, and signs.
Encourage site design that enhances and leverages views to cultural landscapes.
Encourage appropriate edge treatments, transitions, and buffers through site design and development standards for development adjacent to Open Space.
Plat single-loaded streets to maintain scenic edges next to Open Space.
Encourage reconstruction and revegetation to a natural setting.
See Policies 11.3.2 through 11.3.6 below for more about specific cultural landscapes.
See Community Identity Goals 4.1 and 4.3 for character protections and character-defining elements of each Community Planning Area.
See Land Use Policies 5.1.1, 5.3.3, and 5.3.4 for desired development patterns that help preserve natural and cultural features.
See Transportation Goal 6.8 for policies on environmental and cultural considerations in roadway planning, design, and construction.
See Urban Design Goal 7.6 for policies on context-sensitive site design.
See also Resilience & Sustainability Policy 13.4.4 for protection of unique landforms and habitats.

policies.policyDescription: Arroyos: Preserve and enhance arroyos identified in the Rank 2 Facility Plan for Arroyos as important cultural landscapes. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Urban Design Policy 7.6.1 for design considerations for drainage systems.
See Parks & Open Space Policy 10.4.4 on linear Open Space, trails, and acequias.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policies 12.1.4 and 12.1.5 for additional policies on drainage systems.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Policy 12.4.5 for Rank 2 Facility Plans.

policies.policyDescription: Bosque: Regulate development on adjacent lands to preserve and enhance the Bosque as an important cultural landscape that contributes to the history and distinct identity of the region, as well as nearby neighborhoods. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Minimize grading, changes to natural topography, and land disturbance to preserve natural features.
Encourage reconstruction and revegetation to a natural setting on lands adjacent to the Bosque.
Assure compatible land uses and promote cluster development on lands adjacent to the Bosque.
Ensure appropriate edge treatments, transitions, and buffers through site design and development standards.
Encourage links to established public access points to the Bosque from adjacent development.
See Policy 11.3.1 above for view preservation.
See Transportation Goal 6.8 for policies on environmental and cultural considerations in roadway planning, design, and construction.
See Parks & Open Space Policy 10.3.4 for policies related to protecting Open Space lands within the Bosque.
See also Resilience & Sustainability Policy 13.4.4 for protection of unique landforms and habitats.

policies.policyDescription: Petroglyph National Monument: Regulate adjacent development to protect and preserve the Petroglyph National Monument � its volcanoes, petroglyphs, and Northwest Mesa Escarpment � as a priceless cultural landscape and community resource that provides physical, cultural, and economic benefits. [A]

subPolicies.text: Protect the archaeological and historical resources of the Monument.
Preserve and protect the Monument from growth and development pressures on the West Side.
Conserve and protect the Monument and surrounding lands through regulations associated with the Volcano Mesa and Northwest Mesa Escarpment Areas.
Minimize and mitigate negative impacts, including fugitive dust; stormwater runoff; and damage to vegetation, slopes, or boulders.
Follow best practices for blasting to minimize negative impacts and fugitive dust on the Monument.
Minimize visual impact of adjacent development through design standards related to color, reflectivity, building materials, and screening.
Protect views to and from the black Escarpment face, which gives physical order to the community and acts as a visual reference point.
Buffer MPOS and the Monument from adjacent development, preferably with a single-loaded street (i.e. a street with development only on the side not abutting the Monument

subLevels.description: and/or landscaped areas.

subPolicies.text: Prioritize dedication and acquisition of land abutting multi-use trails, rock outcroppings, and the Monument for parks and/or MPOS.
Provide public access at points least sensitive to use and least disruptive to adjacent neighborhoods.
Encourage appropriate edge treatments, transitions, and buffers through site design and development.
See Policy 11.3.1 above for view protection and development that blends with the landscape.
See Policy 11.3.2 above for arroyos.
See Policy 11.3.6 below for policies related to private development within Volcano Mesa.
See Goal 11.4 below for protection of archaeological and paleontological resources.
See Transportation Goal 6.8 for policies on environmental and cultural considerations in roadway planning, design, and construction.
See Parks & Open Space Policy 10.3.5 for protection of land adjacent to the Monument as Open Space.

policies.policyDescription: Sandia Mountains: Protect views of the Sandia Mountains from key vantages within public rights-of-way, along corridors, and from strategic locations as an important cultural feature of the region. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Policy 11.3.1 above for view protection and development that blends with the landscape.
See Transportation Goal 6.8 for policies on environmental and cultural considerations in roadway planning, design, and construction.

policies.policyDescription: Volcano Mesa: Preserve open space, natural and cultural landscapes, and other features of the natural environment within Volcano Mesa. [A]

subPolicies.text: Respect Albuquerque�s culture and history, including Hispanic and Native American, through context-sensitive development.
Encourage development that is sensitive to the open, natural character of the area and geological and cultural conditions.
Protect important views, vistas, and view corridors from within Volcano Mesa to the Rio Grande basin, across the city of Albuquerque, and to the Sandia Mountains.

Protect the area�s natural and archaeological resources, including the Monument and significant rock outcroppings, while encouraging urban development in the Volcano Heights Urban Center to create a vibrant, walkable district with an identity, character, and sense of place inextricably linked to the volcanic landscape.
Limit impermeable surfaces and avoid concentrating stormwater, except through the use of naturalized swales.
Encourage cluster development to preserve undeveloped areas to be used for recreation and/or open space or to protect sensitive land and significant cultural or natural features.

Encourage development that blends with the surrounding landscape, including building colors in harmony with the desert palette, stucco walls or coyote fencing, and native vegetation wherever landscaping is visible to the public from the public right-of-way.
Encourage shared usable open space and park development to be accessible to the public and to connect to adjacent MPOS or the Monument, preserving wildlife corridors and encouraging active living.
See Policy 11.3.1 above for view protection and development that blends with the landscape.
See Policy 11.3.4 above about minimizing physical impacts of development on the Monument.
See Land Use Policies 5.1.1, 5.3.3, and 5.3.4 for desired development patterns that help preserve natural and cultural features.
See Transportation Goal 6.8 for policies on environmental and cultural considerations in roadway planning, design, and construction.

goalDetails.detail: Identify, acquire, and manage significant archaeological and paleontological sites for research, education, tourism, and recreational use.

policies.policyDescription: Archaeological Setting: Consider archaeological and historical resources in relationship to their setting and to each other in terms of determining their significance, appropriate treatment and preservation, appropriate management, and appropriate access and educational opportunities. [A]
Proactive Protection: Identify, evaluate, and protect archaeological and paleontological sites and items on a proactive, ongoing basis. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Policy 11.3.4 above related to the Petroglyph National Monument.
See Policy 11.3.6 above for protections within Volcano Mesa.
See Policy 11.4.5 below for protecting archaeological resources on private land.
See Parks & Open Space Goal 10.3 for policies related to Open Space acquisition.

policies.policyDescription: Archaeological Education: Promote public understanding of and appreciation for the area�s vast archaeological and paleontological resources. [ABC]

Archaeological Ordinance: Protect archaeological resources in the City and the County with regulations for both public and private projects without imposing an undue burden on private property rights. [ABC]

Private Protections: Encourage the private protection of sensitive lands, such as rock outcrops or significant cultural, archaeological, volcanic, or geologic land through private conservation easements, or replatting as private open space. [A]

subPolicies.text: Protect archaeological resources and rock outcroppings on the Northwest Mesa through in-place avoidance, if possible, or mitigation.

Prioritize the conservation of rock art on the Northwest Mesa Escarpment and related archaeological sites to allow further research and discoveries of the people who inhabited the Middle Rio Grande Valley.
See Policy 11.3.4 above for policies related to land adjacent to the Petroglyph National Monument.
See Policy 11.3.6 above for land development in Volcano Mesa.
See Land Use Policy 5.7.2 for regulatory frameworks that protect sensitive lands.

goalDetails.detail: Emphasize and support cultural traditions and arts as vital components of the community�s identities and well-being.

policies.policyDescription: Arts Promotion: Coordinate and promote the arts and the cultural sector as key assets in a vital economy; major contributors to quality of life and healthy communities; and the heart of high-quality, special places that contribute to the identity of distinct communities. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Community Identity Goal 4.3 for arts and culture assets in each Community Planning Area.
See Land Use Policy 5.1.3 for Downtown as an arts destination.

policies.policyDescription: Cultural Facilities: Provide and maintain high-quality public, cultural, and arts facilities to best serve the public. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: Locate new public, cultural, and arts facilities in Centers to best provide access to residents.
Provide arts and cultural facilities equitably throughout the community.
See Infrastructure, Community Facilities & Services Goal 12.2 for policies on community facilities and services.

policies.policyDescription: Cultural Programs: Promote programs that encourage greater understanding of area history and cultural traditions. [ABC]

subPolicies.text: See Goal 11.1 above for policies related to local traditions and heritage.
See Goal 11.2 above for policies about historic assets.
See Policy 11.3.4 above related to programming for Petroglyph National Monument.

policies.policyDescription: Connecting Cultural Centers: Foster better connections among cultural centers and districts, including the National Hispanic Cultural Center, Indian Pueblo Cultural Center, Downtown, and Old Town. [A]

subPolicies.text: See Policy 11.1.4 above for local traditions and heritage.
